这是费曼著名图表的一个独特范例，由费曼本人题写并签名；图片由威廉姆斯学院菲尔德纪念天文学教授杰伊·M·帕萨乔夫提供，该图表绘制在他第一版《量子电动力学》的扉页上。

A unique example of one of Feynman’s famous diagrams, inscribed and signed by Feynman himself; image appears courtesy of Jay M. Pasachoff, Field Memorial Professor of Astronomy at Williams College, for whom the diagram was drawn in the front pages of his first-edition copy of QED.

2006年版导言

Introduction to the 2006 Edition

我们如何认识光的故事本身就是一个扣人心弦的戏剧，充满了曲折离奇的情节和命运的逆转。

The story of how we came to know light makes for one gripping drama, complete with twists and turns and reversals of fortune.

光子是所有基本粒子中最容易观察到的：想象一下，在一个阳光明媚的日子里，你身处一间布满灰尘、只开着一扇小窗户的房间，就能看到无数光子在房间里飞速穿梭。牛顿很自然地认为光是由粒子流（“微粒”）构成的，但他当时也有些疑虑；早在十七世纪，人们就很容易观察到光的衍射现象。最终，衍射和其他一些现象似乎无可辩驳地证明，光是一种电磁波。十九世纪物理学的里程碑——麦克斯韦电磁方程——将光完全描述为一种波动。随后，爱因斯坦出现，他假设光是由无数个能量包（“量子”）组成，以此解释了光电效应。“光子”一词和光的量子理论由此诞生。 （这里我不再赘述爱因斯坦对量子力学的著名抵触情绪，尽管他曾参与量子力学的创立。）与此同时，从20世纪20年代到40年代，物理学家们彻底研究了物质（“原子”）的量子行为。因此，光的量子行为及其与电子的相互作用却始终未能被最杰出、最聪明的科学家所理解，这更令人费解，尤其是保罗·狄拉克和恩里科·费米。物理学界不得不等待三位充满乐观精神的年轻人——费曼、施温格和友野贺——的出现。以及（视情况而定的）悲观情绪，从他们在第二次世界大战中的经历中汲取灵感，从而得出量子电动力学（又称 QED）的正确表述。

The photon is the most visible of all elementary particles: place yourself in a dusty room with one small window open on a sunny day and watch a multitude of the little buggers hurrying across the room. Newton quite naturally thought that light consisted of a stream of particles (“corpuscles”), but already he had some doubts; even in the seventeenth century, the diffraction of light could be readily observed. Eventually, diffraction and other phenomena appeared to show without doubt that light is an electromagnetic wave. That monument of nineteenth-century physics, Maxwell’s equations of electromagnetism, formulated light entirely as a wave. Then Einstein came along and explained the photoelectric effect by postulating light as the sum of little packets (“quanta”) of energy. Thus were the word “photon” and the quantum theory of light born. (Here I will not digress and recall Einstein’s famous discomfort with quantum mechanics, even though he helped at its birth.) Meanwhile, from the 1920s through the 1940s physicists worked out the quantum behavior of matter (“atoms”) thoroughly. Thus, it was all the more puzzling that the quantum behavior of light and its interaction with electrons resisted the efforts of the best and the brightest, notably Paul Dirac and Enrico Fermi. Physics had to wait for three young men—Feynman, Schwinger, and Tomonoga—filled with optimism and pessimism, as the case may be, from their experiences in World War II, to produce the correct formulation of quantum electrodynamics, aka QED.

理查德·费曼（1918-1988）不仅是一位杰出的物理学家，更是一位非凡的人物，他那桀骜不驯的个性，在理论物理学史上前所未见，也后无来者。理论物理学家们偶尔会闲来无事，比较费曼和朱利安·施温格的贡献。他们都是来自纽约的犹太裔年轻人，而且几乎是同时代的人。这种无谓的讨论毫无意义，但事实是，朱利安·施温格性格腼腆内向（但外表冷漠的背后却隐藏着一颗热情善良的心），而迪克·费曼则是一位极度外向的人，堪称传奇人物。他带着邦戈鼓、歌舞女郎，精心打造了一个光鲜亮丽的形象，并受到众多崇拜者的热情追捧。毫无疑问，他是仅次于爱因斯坦的最受欢迎的理论物理学家。

Richard Feynman (1918–1988) was not only an extraordinary physicist, but also an extraordinary figure, a swash-buckling personality the likes of which theoretical physics has not seen before or hence. Occasionally theoretical physicists will while away an idle moment comparing the contributions of Feynman and Schwinger, both nice Jewish boys from New York and almost exact contemporaries. This senseless discussion serves no purpose, but it is a fact that while Julian Schwinger was a shy and retiring person (but rather warm and good-hearted behind his apparent remoteness), Dick Feynman was an extreme extrovert, the stuff of legends. With his bongo drums, showgirls, and other trappings of a carefully cultivated image enthusiastically nurtured by a legion of idolaters, he is surely the best-loved theoretical physicist next to Einstein.

杰出的俄罗斯物理学家列夫·朗道曾提出一个著名的对数尺度来给理论物理学家排名，爱因斯坦位居榜首。众所周知，朗道在提出相变理论后，将自己的排名提升了半位。我也有自己的一套“趣味性”标准，用来衡量我认识的理论物理学家，无论他们是亲身经历还是精神上的敬仰。没错，大多数理论物理学家都枯燥乏味得像白开水，在这个对数尺度上几乎垫底。我会把薛定谔（关于他，我们稍后再谈）放在第一位，费曼肯定紧随其后。我无法告诉你我在自己的标准上排在第几位，但我会尽力享受其中的乐趣，尽管我的才能和资源有限。

The brilliant Russian physicist Lev Landau famously had a logarithmic scale for ranking theoretical physicists, with Einstein on top. It is also well known that Landau moved himself up half a step after he formulated the theory of phase transitions. I have my own scale, one of fun, on which I place theoretical physicists I know either in person or in spirit. Yes, it is true: most theoretical physicists are dull as dishwater and rank near minus infinity on this logarithmic scale. I would place Schrödinger (about whom more later) on top, but Feynman would surely rank close behind. I can’t tell you where I land on my own scale, but I do try to have as much fun as possible, limited by the amount of talent and resources at my disposal.

但费曼真是个有趣的人！在我职业生涯早期，费曼邀请我去夜总会。费曼的一位同事告诉我，这个邀请表明他认真对待我作为一名物理学家的身份，但我当时虽然很想去，却没能如愿。告诉费曼我对杨-米尔斯理论的看法，他只想知道我对舞台上舞女腿的看法。当然，在英雄崇拜的心理学中，没人会在乎一个只会打鼓、喜欢歌舞女郎的物理学家。所以，好吧，我的评判标准是“玩乐天赋”——兰道的评判标准加上了玩乐因素，爱因斯坦的地位下降，兰道的地位上升（他玩得很开心，直到被克格勃抓走）。

But what fun Feynman was! Early in my career, Feynman asked me to go to a nightclub with him. One of Feynman’s colleagues told me that the invitation showed that he took me seriously as a physicist, but while I was eager to tell Feynman my thoughts about Yang-Mills theory, he only wanted my opinion on the legs of the dancing girls on stage. Of course, in the psychology of hero worship, nobody gives two hoots about some bozo of a physicist who plays drums and likes showgirls. So all right, my scale is really fun times talent—Landau’s scale with fun factored in, with the stock of Einstein falling and that of Landau rising (he played some good pranks until the KGB got him).

如今，距离那次夜总会之行已过去三十余年，普林斯顿大学出版社的英格丽德·格内利希邀请我为费曼的著名著作《量子电动力学：光与物质的奇异理论》 （2006年版）撰写导言，我深感荣幸。首先坦白：我之前从未读过《量子电动力学》。这本书于1985年出版时，我刚刚完成我的第一本科普物理著作《恐惧的对称性》，之后我便奉行着不读其他科普物理书籍的原则，唯恐它们影响我的写作风格。因此，我带着全新的视角和深深的欣赏阅读了英格丽德寄给我的样书。我非常享受阅读的过程，并一边阅读一边记下我的想法和评论。

Now some thirty years after that night club visit, I felt honored that Ingrid Gnerlich of Princeton University Press should ask me to write an introduction to the 2006 edition of Feynman’s famous book QED: The Strange Theory of Light and Matter. First a confession: I had never read QED before. When this book came out in 1985 I had just finished writing my first popular physics book, Fearful Symmetry, and I more or less adopted a policy of not reading other popular physics books for fear of their influencing my style. Thus, I read the copy Ingrid sent me with fresh eyes and deep appreciation. I enjoyed it immensely, jotting down my thoughts and critiques as I went along.

我之前没读过这本书真是个错误，因为它并非通常意义上的科普物理书籍。1984年，史蒂夫·温伯格建议我写一本科普物理书籍，并安排我在纽约与他的编辑见面。他给了我一条很有价值的建议。他说，大多数写这类书的物理学家都忍不住想要解释一切，而普通读者只想获得一种理解的错觉，并记住一些可以在鸡尾酒会上炫耀的流行词汇。

I was wrong not to have read this book before, because it is not a popular physics book in the usual sense of the phrase. When Steve Weinberg suggested in 1984 that I write a popular physics book and arranged for me to meet his editor in New York, he gave me a useful piece of advice. He said that most physicists who wrote such books could not resist the urge of explaining everything, while the lay reader only wanted to have the illusion of understanding and to catch a few buzzwords to throw around at cocktail parties.

我认为温伯格的观点虽然有些愤世嫉俗，但总体上是正确的。霍金的《时间简史》的巨大成功就是一个例证（根据我之前提到的计划，我还没有读过这本书）。我在加州大学的前同事，一位现任牛津大学教授的杰出物理学家，曾给我看过那本书里的一句话。我们俩试图理解这句话的意思，但都失败了。相比之下，我想向所有感到困惑的读者保证，这本书里的每一句话，虽然看起来极其怪异，但都是有道理的。不过，你必须仔细琢磨每一句话，努力理解费曼的意思，然后再继续阅读。否则，我保证你会彻底迷失方向。真正怪异的是物理学本身，而不是它的表达方式。毕竟，书名就预示着一个“奇异的理论”。

I think that Weinberg’s view, though somewhat cynical, is largely correct. Witness the phenomenal success of Hawking’s A Brief History of Time (which I have not read in accordance with the policy I mentioned earlier). One of my former colleagues here at the University of California, a distinguished physicist who now holds a chair at Oxford, once showed me a sentence from that book. The two of us tried to make sense of it and failed. In contrast, I want to assure all the puzzled readers that every sentence in this book, though seemingly bizarre to the max, makes sense. But you must mull over each sentence carefully and try hard to understand what Feynman is saying before moving on. Otherwise, I guarantee that you will be hopelessly lost. It is the physics that is bizarre, not the presentation. After all, the title promises a “strange theory.”

费曼毕竟是费曼，他完全违背了温伯格给我的建议（顺便一提，我也没有完全遵循他的建议；参见下文关于群论的评论）。在致谢部分，费曼抨击科普物理书籍“表面上的简洁只是通过描述一些与它们声称描述的截然不同的东西，一些与它们声称描述的截然不同的东西”。相反，他给自己设定了一个挑战：如何在不“歪曲事实”的情况下，向普通读者描述量子电动力学。因此，你不应该把这本书看作是一本典型的科普物理书籍，也不是一本教科书。它实际上是一种罕见的混合体。

Since Feynman was Feynman, he chose to go totally against the advice Weinberg gave me (advice which I incidentally also did not follow completely; see my remark below regarding group theory). In the acknowledgment, Feynman decried popular physics books as achieving “apparent simplicity only by describing something different, something considerably distorted from what they claim to be describing.” Instead, he posed himself the challenge of describing QED to the lay reader without “distortion of the truth.” Thus, you should not think of this book as a typical popular physics book. Neither is it a textbook. A rare hybrid it is instead.

为了解释这本书的类型，我将借用费曼的比喻，并稍作修改。费曼认为，学习量子电动力学只有两种选择：要么接受七年的物理教育，要么阅读这本书。（他的数字有点高估了；如今，一个聪明的高中毕业生，只要有合适的指导，可能不到七年就能掌握。）所以你其实别无选择，不是吗？你当然应该选择阅读这本书！即使你像我建议的那样仔细研读每一句话，也不需要花费七周时间，更别说七年了。

To explain what kind of book this is, I will use Feynman’s own analogy, somewhat modified. According to Feynman, to learn QED you have two choices: you can either go through seven years of physics education or read this book. (His figure is a bit of an overestimate; these days a bright high-school graduate with the proper guidance could probably do it in less than seven years.) So you don’t really have a choice, do you? Of course you should choose to read this book! Even if you mull over every sentence as I suggest you do, it should not take you seven weeks, let alone seven years.

那么这两种选择有何不同呢？现在我来举个例子：一位玛雅大祭司宣布：只要付钱，他就能教你——一个玛雅社会里的普通人——如何计算两个数的乘法，比如564乘以253。他会让你背一个9乘9的乘法表，然后告诉你，你要看两个要乘的数中最右边的两位数字，也就是4和3，并说出乘法表第四行第三列的数字。你说12。然后你就会学到，你应该写下2，然后“进位”1，不管这是什么意思。接下来，你要说出第六行第三列的数字，也就是18，然后加上你进位的数字。当然，你还得再花一年时间学习如何“加法”。好吧，你明白我的意思了。这就是你在名牌大学交了学费之后会学到的东西。

So how do these two choices differ? Now comes my version of the analogy: a Mayan high priest announces that for a fee he could teach you, an ordinary Joe or Jane in Mayan society, how to multiply two numbers, for example 564 by 253. He makes you memorize a 9-by-9 table and then tells you to look at the two digits farthest to the right in the two numbers you have to multiply, namely, 4 and 3, and say what is in the 4th row and 3rd column of the table. You say 12. Then you learn that you should write down 2 and “carry” 1, whatever that means. Next you are to say what is in the 6th row and 3rd column, namely, 18, to which you are told to add the number you are carrying. Of course, you’d have to spend another year learning how to “add.” Well, you get the idea. This is what you would learn after paying tuition at a prestigious university.

相反，一个名叫费曼的聪明人走过来对你说：“嘘，如果你会数数，就不用学那些复杂的进位和加法了！你只需要准备564个罐子。然后每个罐子里放253颗鹅卵石。最后，把所有的鹅卵石都倒出来堆成一大堆，数一数。答案就在这里！”

Instead, a wise guy named Feynman approaches you saying, “Shh, if you know how to count, you don’t have to learn all this fancy stuff about carrying and adding! All you’ve got to do is to get a hold of 564 jars. Then you put into each jar 253 pebbles. Finally, you pour all the pebbles out onto a big pile and count them. That’s the answer!”

所以你看，费曼不仅教你如何乘法，还让你深刻理解那些即将从名牌大学获得博士学位的“大祭司”及其学生究竟在做什么！另一方面，如果你学的是费曼的乘法方法，你可能就没法去应聘会计的工作了。如果你的老板让你整天计算大数乘法，你会累得筋疲力尽，而那些从“大祭司大学”毕业的学生会把你远远甩在身后。

So you see, Feynman not only teaches you how to multiply, but also gives you a deep understanding of what the high priests and their students, those people soon to have Ph.D.s from prestigious universities, are doing! On the other hand, if you learn to multiply Feynman’s way, you couldn’t quite apply for a job as an accountant. If your boss asked you to multiply big numbers all day long, you would be exhausted, and the students who went to High Priest University would leave you in the dust.

我既写过一本教科书（《量子场论简明教程》，以下简称《简明教程》），也写过两本科普物理书籍（包括《令人恐惧的对称性》 ，以下简称《令人恐惧的对称性》），所以我觉得自己完全有资格回答您关于应该读哪些书的问题。（顺便一提，本书的出版商普林斯顿大学出版社也出版了《简明教程》和《令人恐惧的对称性》。）

Having written both a textbook (Quantum Field Theory in a Nutshell, henceforth referred to as Nutshell) and two popular physics books (including Fearful Symmetry, henceforth Fearful), I feel that I am quite qualified to address your concerns about what kinds of books to read. (By the way, Princeton University Press, the publisher of this book, publishes both Nutshell and Fearful.)

让我把这篇引言的读者分成三类。目标读者：（1）受本书启发而继续学习并掌握量子电动力学的学生；（2）对量子电动力学感兴趣的有见识的普通人；（3）像我这样的专业物理学家。

Let me divide the readers of this introduction into three classes: (1) students who may be inspired by this book to go on and master QED, (2) intelligent laypersons curious about QED, and (3) professional physicists like myself.

如果你是大一新生，这本书会给你带来巨大的启发和热情，让你迫不及待地想要去读一本量子场论的教科书（而且很可能就是《量子场论精要》！）。顺便一提，如今量子电动力学被认为是一个相对简单的量子场论例子。在撰写《量子场论精要》时，我认为一个真正聪明的本科生完全有机会理解量子场论，费曼肯定也会同意我的观点。

If you are in class 1, you will be so incredibly inspired and fired up by this book that you will want to rush out and start reading a textbook on quantum field theory (and it might as well be Nutshell!) By the way, these days QED is considered a relatively simple example of a quantum field theory. In writing Nutshell, I contend that a truly bright undergrad would have a good shot at understanding quantum field theory, and Feynman would surely agree with me.

但正如前面的比喻，单靠阅读这本书并不能让你成为专家。你必须学习费曼所说的“巧妙而高效的”乘法方法。尽管费曼声称要从头开始解释一切，但随着论述的深入，他明显后劲不足。例如，在第 89 页和图 56中，他仅仅描述了 P(A 到 B) 对“区间I ”的奇特依赖关系，而你只能相信他的话。在《数学简述》中，这一点得到了推导。同样，第 91 页脚注中描述的量 E(A 到 B) 也是如此。

But as in the analogy, reading this book alone will in no way turn you into a pro. You have to learn what Feynman referred to as the “tricky, efficient way” of multiplying numbers. In spite of Feynman’s proclaimed desire to explain everything from scratch, he noticeably runs out of steam as he goes on. For example, on page 89 and in figure 56, he merely describes the bizarre dependence of P(A to B) on the “interval I” and you just have to take his word for it. In Nutshell, this is derived. Similarly for the quantity E(A to B) described in the footnote on page 91.

如果你还在二年级，坚持下去，你会有收获的，相信我。别着急。即使你只读完了前两章，你也会学到很多东西。为什么这本书这么难读呢？我们可以回到玛雅人的比喻：这就像你教别人乘法，给他讲罐子和鹅卵石，但他甚至不知道罐子和鹅卵石是什么。费曼一会儿告诉你每个光子都带着一个小箭头，一会儿告诉你如何把这些箭头加起来、乘起来、缩小、旋转。这一切都非常令人困惑；你连一丝一毫的注意力都不能分散。顺便说一句，这些小箭头其实就是复数（正如第63页脚注所解释的），如果你已经……如果你了解复数（以及罐子和鹅卵石），那么讨论可能就不会那么令人困惑了。或者，你可能属于温伯格所描述的那种典型的非专业读者，满足于“理解事物的错觉”。在这种情况下，一本“普通”的科普物理书籍或许就能满足你的需求。再次用玛雅人的比喻：一本普通的科普物理书籍既不会让你费力地去计算九宫格和搬运，也不会让你去研究罐子和鹅卵石。它可能只是简单地告诉你，当给定两个数字时，祭司们有办法得出另一个数字。事实上，科普物理书籍的编辑们坚持要求作者这样写作，以免吓跑付费读者（详见下文）。

If you are in class 2, persevere and you will be rewarded, trust me. Don’t rush. Even if you only get through the first two chapters, you will have learned a lot. Why is this book so hard to read? We could go back to the Mayan analogy: it is as if you are teaching someone to multiply by telling him about jars and pebbles, but he doesn’t even know what a jar or a pebble is. Feynman is bouncing around telling you about each photon carrying a little arrow, and about how you add up these arrows and multiply them, shrinking and rotating them. It is all very confusing; you can’t afford even the slightest lapse in attention. Incidentally, the little arrows are just complex numbers (as explained in a footnote on page 63), and if you already know about complex numbers (and jars and pebbles), the discussion might be less confusing. Or perhaps you are one of those typical lay readers described by Weinberg, who are satisfied with “the illusion of understanding something.” In that case, you may be satisfied with a “normal” popular physics book. Again the Mayan analogy: a normal popular physics book would burden you neither with 9-by-9 tables and carrying, nor with jars and pebbles. It might simply say that when given two numbers, the high priests have a way of producing another number. In fact, editors of popular physics books insist that authors write like that in order not to scare away the paying public (more below).

最后，如果你是三年级学生，那你可真是走运了。即使我是一名量子场论学者，了解费曼的理论，看到他用如此新颖独特的方式解释熟悉的现象，我仍然感到非常欣喜。费曼向我解释了为什么光沿直线传播，以及聚焦透镜的真正工作原理（第58页：“可以通过减慢光速来‘戏弄’自然界，使所有小箭头都转动相同的角度！”）。

Finally, if you are in class 3, you are in for a real treat. Even though I am a quantum field theorist and know what Feynman is doing, I still derived great pleasure from seeing familiar phenomena explained in a dazzlingly original and unfamiliar way. I enjoyed having Feynman explain to me why light moves in a straight line or how a focusing lens really works (on page 58: “A ‘trick’ can be played on Nature” by slowing light down along certain paths so the little arrows all turn by the same amount!).

嘘。我来告诉你费曼为什么和大多数物理教授不一样。你去问问哪个物理教授，为什么在玻璃板反射光线时，只需要考虑前表面和后表面的反射就足够了？很少有人知道答案（见第104页）。这并非因为物理教授缺乏知识，而是因为他们从未想过要问这个问题。他们只是研读杰克逊的标准教科书，通过考试，然后就结束了。费曼就像个爱刨根问底的孩子，永远都在问为什么，为什么，为什么！

Shh. I will tell you why Feynman is different from most physics professors. Go ask a physics professor to explain why, in the reflection of light from a pane of glass, it suffices to consider reflection from the front surface and the back surface only. Very few would know the answer (see page 104). It is not because physics professors lack the knowledge, but because it has never even occurred to them to ask this question. They simply study the standard textbook by Jackson, pass the exam, and move on. Feynman is the pesky kid who is forever asking why, WHY, WHY!

根据读者的类别（有抱负的学生、有见识的普通读者、专业人士），物理学书籍也有三类（并非一一对应）：教科书、科普读物，以及我称之为“难度极高的科普物理书籍”的书籍。本书属于第三类，某种程度上介于教科书和科普读物之间，实属罕见。为什么这类书籍如此稀少？因为“难度极高的科普物理书籍”吓坏了出版商。霍金曾说过一句名言：每出现一个公式，科普书籍的销量就会减半。我并不否认这句话的普遍真理，但我希望出版商不要如此轻易被吓倒。问题不在于公式的数量，而在于科普书籍能否如实呈现深奥的概念。我写《恐惧》一书时，认为要讨论现代物理学中的对称性，就必须先解释群论。我尝试用一​​些小符号来解释这些概念：方框和圆圈里都带有字母。但编辑却强迫我反复淡化讨论，直到几乎什么都没剩下，然后又把剩下的大部分内容放到附录里。另一方面，费曼拥有并非所有物理学家作家都能拥有的影响力。

With three classes of readers (the aspiring student, the intelligent layperson, the pro), there are also three categories of physics books (not in one-to-one correspondence): textbooks, popular books, and what I might call “extra-difficult popular physics books.” This book is a rare example of the third category, in some sense intermediate between a textbook and a popular book. Why is this third category so thinly populated? Because “extra-difficult popular physics books” scare publishers half to death. Hawking famously said that every equation halves the sale of a popular book. While I do not deny the general truth of this statement, I wish that publishers would not be so easily frightened. The issue is not so much the number of equations, but whether popular books could contain an honest presentation of difficult concepts. When I wrote Fearful, I thought that to discuss symmetry in modern physics it would be essential to explain group theory. I tried to make the concepts accessible by the use of little tokens: squares and circles with letters inside them. But the editor compelled me to water the discussion down repeatedly until there was practically nothing left, and then to relegate much of what was left to an appendix. Feynman, on the other hand, had the kind of clout that not every physicist-writer would have.

让我回到费曼的书中那些晦涩难懂的段落。这本书的许多读者可能都接触过一些量子物理。因此，他们或许会感到困惑，例如，为什么书中没有提到在其他量子物理科普读物中占据重要地位的波函数。量子物理本身就够令人费解了——正如一位智者曾经说过的那样，“有了量子物理，谁还需要毒品？”或许读者应该免得再为此苦恼。那么，让我来解释一下。

Let me return to Feynman’s book with its difficult passages. Many of the readers of this book will have had some exposure to quantum physics. Therefore, they may be legitimately puzzled, for example, by the absence of the wave function that figures so prominently in other popular discussions of quantum physics. Quantum physics is puzzling enough—as a wit once said, “With quantum physics, who needs drugs?” Perhaps the reader should be spared further head scratching. So let me explain.

几乎同时但各自独立地，埃尔温·薛定谔和维尔纳·海森堡发明了量子力学。例如，为了描述电子的运动，薛定谔引入了一个由偏微分方程控制的波函数，这个方程现在被称为……薛定谔方程。相比之下，海森堡则通过谈论作用于他所谓的“量子态”的算符，令周围的人感到困惑。他还提出了著名的不确定性原理，该原理指出，测量量子粒子位置的精度越高，对其动量的了解就越不确定，反之亦然。

Almost simultaneously but independently, Erwin Schrödinger and Werner Heisenberg invented quantum mechanics. To describe the motion of an electron, for example, Schrödinger introduced a wave function governed by a partial differential equation, now known as the Schrödinger equation. In contrast, Heisenberg mystified those around him by talking about operators acting on what he called “quantum states.” He also famously enunciated the uncertainty principle, which states that the more accurately one were to measure, say, the position of a quantum particle, the more uncertain becomes one’s knowledge of its momentum, and vice versa.

这两位学者建立的形式体系显然不同，但他们对任何物理过程得出的最终结果却总是一致的。后来，人们证明这两种形式体系完全等价。如今，任何合格的研究生都应该能够自如地在两种形式体系之间转换，并根据具体问题的需要灵活运用其中一种。

The formalisms set up by the two men were manifestly different, but the bottom-line result they obtained for any physical process always agreed. Later, the two formalisms were shown to be completely equivalent. Today, any decent graduate student is expected to pass from one formalism to the other with facility, employing one or the other according to which one is more convenient for the problem at hand.

六年后的1932年，保罗·狄拉克以一种略显粗糙的形式提出了第三种形式体系。狄拉克的想法似乎被人们遗忘，直到1941年费曼发展并完善了这一形式体系，它后来被称为路径积分形式体系，或历史求和形式体系。（物理学家有时会怀疑费曼是否在完全不了解狄拉克的工作的情况下发明了这一形式体系。物理史学家现在已经证实答案是否定的。在普林斯顿一家小酒馆的聚会上，一位名叫赫伯特·耶勒的来访物理学家向费曼介绍了狄拉克的想法，显然第二天费曼就在惊叹不已的耶勒面前实时推导出了这一形式体系。参见S. Schweber于1986年发表在《现代物理评论》上的文章。）

Six years later, in 1932, Paul Dirac suggested, in a somewhat rudimentary form, yet a third formalism. Dirac’s idea appeared to be largely forgotten until 1941, when Feynman developed and elaborated this formalism, which became known as the path integral formalism, or sum over history formalism. (Physicists sometimes wonder whether Feynman invented this formalism completely ignorant of Dirac’s work. Historians of physics have now established that the answer is no. During a party at a Princeton tavern, a visiting physicist named Herbert Jehle told Feynman about Dirac’s idea, and apparently the next day Feynman worked out the formalism in real time in front of the awed Jehle. See the 1986 article by S. Schweber in Reviews of Modern Physics.)

费曼在这本小册子中竭力解释的就是这种形式主义。例如，在第 43 页，当费曼把所有箭头加起来时，他实际上是在对光子从 S 点到 P 点所有可能路径的振幅进行积分（这当然是微积分术语，意为求和）。因此有了“积分”这个术语。“路径积分形式主义”。另一个术语“历史求和”也很容易理解。如果量子物理学的规则适用于宏观的人类尺度事务，那么所有可能的替代历史，例如拿破仑在滑铁卢战役中获胜或肯尼迪躲过刺客的子弹，都将是可能的，而每段历史都将与一个振幅相关联，我们需要对这个振幅进行求和（“对所有小箭头求和”）。

It is this formalism that Feynman tries hard to explain in this little book. For example, on page 43, when Feynman adds all those arrows, he is actually integrating (which of course is calculus jargon for summing) over the amplitudes associated with all possible paths the photon could follow in getting from point S to point P. Hence the term “path integral formalism.” The alternative term “sum over history” is also easy to understand. Were the rules of quantum physics relevant to affairs on the macroscopic human scale, then all alternative histories, such as Napoleon triumphing at Waterloo or Kennedy dodging the assassin’s bullet, would be possible, and each history would be associated with an amplitude that we are to sum over (“summing over all those little arrows”).

事实证明，路径积分作为末态的函数，满足薛定谔方程。路径积分本质上就是波函数。因此，路径积分形式与薛定谔形式和海森堡形式完全等价。实际上，唯一一本清晰解释这种等价性的教科书是由费曼和希布斯合著的。（没错，费曼也写过教科书——你知道，就是那种枯燥乏味，但实际上教你如何高效地进行运算，比如“进位”和“加法”的书。而且，没错，你猜对了，费曼的教科书通常大部分是由他的合作者撰写的。）

It turns out that the path integral, regarded as a function of the final state, satisfies the Schrödinger equation. The path integral is essentially the wave function. Hence the path integral formalism is completely equivalent to the Schrödinger and Heisenberg formalisms. In fact, the one textbook that explains this equivalence clearly was written by Feynman and Hibbs. (Yes, Feynman has also authored textbooks—you know, those boring books that actually tell you how to do things efficiently, like “carrying” and “adding.” Also, yes, you guessed correctly that Feynman’s textbooks are often largely written by his coauthors.)

由于狄拉克-费曼路径积分形式与海森堡形式完全等价，它必然包含不确定性原理。因此，费曼在第55页和56页对不确定性原理的轻描淡写未免有些夸张。至少，我们可以就语义问题展开争论：他说不确定性原理“不需要”究竟是什么意思？真正的问题在于它是否真的有用。

Since the Dirac-Feynman path integral formalism is completely equivalent to the Heisenberg formalism, it most certainly contains the uncertainty principle. So Feynman’s cheerful dismissal of the uncertainty principle on pages 55 and 56 is a bit of an exaggeration. At the very least, one can argue over semantics: what did he mean by saying that the uncertainty principle is not “needed”? The real issue is whether or not it is useful.

理论物理学家出了名的务实。他们会选择最简单的方法。他们不像数学家那样固执地坚持严谨和证明。只要有效就行！

Theoretical physicists are a notoriously pragmatic lot. They will use whichever method is the easiest. There is none of the mathematicians’ petulant insistence on rigor and proof. Whatever works, man!

鉴于这种态度，你可能会问，薛定谔方程、海森堡方程和狄拉克-费曼方程这三种形式体系中，哪一种最简单？答案取决于具体问题。例如，在处理原子问题时，正如薛定谔方程大师本人所承认的那样……在第100页，费曼图“对于这些原子来说，会包含太多直线和曲线，简直一团糟！” 而薛定谔方程则简单得多，物理学家们也正是使用薛定谔方程。事实上，对于大多数“实际”问题，路径积分方程几乎复杂到令人绝望，在某些情况下甚至根本无法使用。我曾经就其中一个看似不可能的情况问过费曼，但他也无言以对。然而，初学者使用薛定谔方程却能轻松解决这些看似不可能的问题！

Given this attitude, you may ask, which of the three formalisms—Schrödinger, Heisenberg, or Dirac-Feynman—is the easiest? The answer depends on the problem. In treating atoms, for example, as the master himself admits on page 100, the Feynman diagrams “for these atoms would involve so many straight and wiggly lines that they’d be a complete mess!” The Schrödinger formalism is much easier by a long shot, and that is what physicists use. In fact, for most “practical” problems the path integral formalism is almost hopelessly involved, and in some cases downright impossible to use. I once asked Feynman about one of these apparently impossible cases and he had no answer. Yet, beginning students using the Schrödinger formalism easily solve these apparently impossible cases!

因此，哪种形式体系最佳实际上取决于具体的物理问题，例如，某一领域的理论物理学家（如原子物理学家）可能倾向于某种形式体系，而另一领域的理论物理学家（如高能物理学家）则可能更偏爱另一种形式体系。由此可见，随着某一特定领域的演进和发展，某种形式体系甚至可能比其他形式体系更为便捷。

Thus, which formalism is best really depends on the physics problem, so that theoretical physicists in one field—atomic physics, for example—might favor one formalism, while those in another—such as high energy physics—might prefer a different formalism. Logically then, it may even happen that, as a given field evolves and develops, one formalism may emerge as more convenient than another.

具体来说，我想重点谈谈我受训的领域，即高能物​​理或粒子物理，这也是费曼的主要研究领域。有趣的是，在粒子物理领域，路径积分形式主义长期以来在三种形式主义的竞争中都处于落后地位。（顺便说一句，并没有规定只能有三种形式主义。说不定某个聪明的年轻人会提出第四种！）事实上，路径积分形式主义对于大多数问题来说都非常笨拙，以至于到了20世纪60年代末，它几乎完全被人遗忘。那时，量子场论几乎完全使用正则形式主义进行教学，这其实就是海森堡形式主义的另一种说法，但“正则”这个词本身就足以说明哪种形式主义最受推崇。举一个我比较熟悉的例子，我在学生时代从未听说过路径积分，尽管我上过两所相当知名的大学。我的本科和研究生学习都在东海岸完成。（之所以提到东海岸，是因为据我所知，路径积分很可能在洛杉矶东部的某个地方进行过深入教学。）直到我在普林斯顿高等研究院做博士后时，我和大多数同事才通过一篇俄罗斯论文首次接触到路径积分形式体系。即便如此，当时也有不少权威人士对这一形式体系表示怀疑。

To be specific, let me focus on the field I was trained in, namely, high energy, or particle, physics, which is also Feynman’s main field. Interestingly, in particle physics the path integral formalism for a long time ran a distant third in the horse race between the three formalisms. (By the way, nothing says that there could be only three. Some bright young guy could very well come up with a fourth!) In fact, the path integral formalism was so unwieldy for most problems that by the late 1960s it almost fell into complete obscurity. By that time, quantum field theory was almost exclusively taught using the canonical formalism, which is merely another word for the Heisenberg formalism, but the very word “canonical” should tell you which formalism was held in the highest esteem. To cite just one case history I happen to know well, I had never heard of the path integral during my student days, even though I went to two reasonably reputable universities on the East Coast for my undergraduate and graduate studies. (I mention the East Coast because, for all I know, the path integral could have been taught intensively in an eastern enclave in Los Angeles.) It was not until I was a postdoc at the Institute for Advanced Study that I, as well as most of my colleagues, was first alerted to the path integral formalism by a Russian paper. Even then, various authorities expressed doubts about the formalism.

讽刺的是，造成这种令人遗憾的局面，正是费曼本人一手造成的。事情是这样的：学生们轻而易举地学会了费曼发明的那些“有趣的小图”（比如第116页上的那些）。朱利安·施温格曾颇为愤慨地说道：“费曼把量子场论带给了大众”，他的意思是，任何一个笨蛋只要记住几条“费曼规则”，就能自称是场论专家，并以此谋生。一代又一代人学习费曼图，却对场论一无所知。天哪，现在居然还有这样的大学教授在世上！

Ironically, it was Feynman himself who was responsible for this deplorable state of affairs. What happened was that students easily learned the “funny little diagrams” (such as those on page 116) invented by Feynman. Julian Schwinger once said rather bitterly that “Feynman brought quantum field theory to the masses,” by which he meant that any dullard could memorize a few “Feynman rules,” call himself or herself a field theorist, and build a credible career. Generations learned Feynman diagrams without understanding field theory. Heavens to Betsy, there are still university professors like that walking around!

但是，几乎令人难以置信的是——或许这正是费曼身上那层神秘色彩的一部分，赋予了他的职业生涯一种近乎神奇的光环——在20世纪70年代初，主要从我刚才提到的那篇俄罗斯论文开始，狄拉克-费曼路径积分强势回归。它迅速成为量子场论研究取得进展的主要方法。

But then, almost incredibly—and perhaps this is part of the Feynman mystique that gave his career an almost magical aura—in the early 1970s, starting largely with that Russian paper I just mentioned, the Dirac-Feynman path integral made a roaring comeback. It quickly became the dominant way to make progress in quantum field theory.

费曼之所以是一位杰出的物理学家，是因为他刚才所说的这场“争夺人心”的斗争，实际上是在使用费曼图的一代人与使用费曼路径积分的新一代人之间展开的。不过，我得赶紧补充一句，“斗争”这个词用得有点过了：物理学家完全可以同时使用这两种方法。就拿我来说，我就同时使用了这两种方法。

What makes Feynman such an extraordinary physicist is that this “battle for the hearts and minds” I just described was between the crowd using Feynman diagrams versus a younger crowd using Feynman path integrals. I hasten to add that the word “battle” is a bit strong: nothing prevents a physicist from using both. I did, for one.

我相信我最近出版的教科书《Nutshell》是少数从一开始就采用路径积分形式的教科书之一，这与一些早期的教科书形成鲜明对比，后者往往倾向于使用其他方法。规范形式主义。我在第二章开头写了一节，标题是“教授的噩梦：班里有个自作聪明的学生”。本着那些关于费曼的轶事精神，我编造了一个关于自作聪明的学生的故事，并给他取名为费曼。路径积分形式主义的推导过程颇具禅意：引入无限多个屏幕，并在每个屏幕上钻无限多个孔，最终得到一个没有屏幕的屏幕。但就像玛雅祭司的比喻一样，在费曼式的推导之后，我不得不教学生如何实际计算（“进位”和“加法”），为此我不得不放弃费曼的轶事，转而详细讲解狄拉克-费曼对路径积分形式主义的推导，并引入诸如“将1插入到一组完整的左矢和右矢的和中”之类的技术细节。费曼的书里可没有这些技术细节！

I believe that my recent textbook Nutshell is one of the few that employ the path integral formalism right from the beginning, in contrast to older textbooks that favor the canonical formalism. I started the second chapter with a section titled “The professor’s nightmare: a wise guy in the class.” In the spirit of all those apocryphal stories about Feynman, I made up a story about a wise-guy student and named him Feynman. The path integral formalism was derived by the rather Zen procedure of introducing an infinite number of screens and drilling an infinite number of holes in each screen, thus ending up with no screen. But as in the Mayan priesthood analogy, after this Feynmanesque derivation, I had to teach the student how to actually calculate (“carry” and “add”) and for that I had to abandon the apocryphal Feynman and go through the detailed Dirac-Feynman derivation of the path integral formalism, introducing such technicalities as “the insertion of 1 as a sum over a complete set of bras and kets.” Technicality is what you do not get by reading Feynman’s books!

顺便一提，如果你好奇的话，胸罩跟风流成性的迪克·费曼一点关系都没有。它们是由沉稳寡言的保罗·狄拉克引入的，当时他把胸罩当作括号的左半部分。狄拉克本人就是个传奇人物：我曾经和狄拉克等人一起吃过一顿饭，他全程一句话都没说。

Incidentally, in case you are wondering, the bras have nothing to do with the philandering Dick Feynman. They were introduced by the staid and laconic Paul Dirac as the left half of a bracket. Dirac is himself a legend: I once sat through an entire dinner with Dirac and others without him uttering more than a few words.

费曼不时地暗讽其他物理学家，我忍不住笑了好几次。比如，在第132页，他轻蔑地称默里·盖尔曼——这位才华横溢的物理学家，也是他在加州理工学院的友好竞争对手——为“伟大的发明家”。这与他精心塑造的机智幽默的形象略有出入，他在第135页哀叹物理学家们对希腊语的掌握普遍下降，而他心里很清楚，盖尔曼不仅创造了“胶子”（gluon）这个新词，而且还是一位精通语言学的学者。

I chuckled a few times as Feynman got in some sly digs at other physicists. For example, on page 132 he dismissively referred to Murray Gell-Mann, the brilliant physicist and Feynman’s friendly rival at Caltech, as a “great inventor.” Going somewhat against his own carefully cultivated wise-guy image, he then deplored on page 135 the general decline of physicists’ knowledge of Greek, knowing full well that Gell-Mann not only coined the neologism “gluon” but is also an accomplished linguist.

我也很喜欢费曼的自嘲，这正是他形象不可或缺的一部分。在第149页，费曼谈到“某个愚蠢的物理学家在……发表演讲”时，1983 年的加州大学洛杉矶分校”，有些读者可能没意识到费曼说的就是他自己！虽然这确实是这幅画面的一部分，但我觉得它令人耳目一新，因为我们这些理论物理学家如今越来越等级森严、自命不凡。我认识的费曼——我强调一下，我跟他并不熟——肯定不会喜欢这种风气。毕竟，他曾经为了辞去美国国家科学院院士的职务而闹出过轩然大波。

I also liked Feynman’s self-deprecatory remarks, which are part and parcel of his image. On page 149, when Feynman speaks of “some fool physicist giv[ing] a lecture at UCLA in 1983,” some readers might not realize that Feynman is speaking of himself! Although this is indeed part of the image, I find it refreshing as we theoretical physicists become increasingly hierarchical and pompous in our time. The Feynman whom I knew—and I emphasize that I did not know him well—surely would not like this trend. Afterall, he once caused a big fuss trying to resign from the National Academy of Sciences.

回顾我上面提到的三类潜在读者，我认为第二类和第三类读者会非常喜欢这本书，但这本书实际上是为第一类读者写的。如果你是一位有抱负的理论物理学家，我强烈建议你带着你心中那股强烈的求知欲去阅读这本书，然后再去学习量子场论教科书，看看如何真正地“运用”理论。

Referring back to the three classes of potential readers I described above, I would say that those in classes 2 and 3 will enjoy this book enormously, but the book was secretly written for those in class 1. If you are an aspiring theoretical physicist, I urge you to devour this book with all the fiery hunger you feel in your mind, and then go on to learn from a quantum field theory textbook how to actually “carry.”

你肯定能掌握量子场论。记住费曼说过的话：“一个傻瓜能理解的东西，另一个傻瓜也能理解。”他指的就是他自己，也指你！

Surely you can master quantum field theory. Just remember what Feynman said: “What one fool can understand, another can.” He was referring to himself, and to you!

A. Z EE

A. ZEE

前言

Foreword

艾莉克斯·G·莫特纳纪念讲座是为了纪念我的妻子艾莉克斯而设立的，她于1982年去世。虽然她的职业生涯专注于英国文学，但她对许多科学领域一直抱有浓厚的兴趣。因此，我觉得以她的名义设立一个基金，用于支持年度系列讲座，旨在向有识之士和求知欲的公众传播科学的精神和成就，是再合适不过的了。

The Alix G. Mautner Memorial Lectures were conceived in honor of my wife Alix, who died in 1982. Although her career was in English literature, Alix had a long and abiding interest in many scientific fields. Thus it seemed fitting to create a fund in her name that would support an annual lecture series with the objective of communicating to an intelligent and interested public the spirit and achievements of science.

我很高兴理查德·费曼同意举办首场系列讲座。我们的友谊可以追溯到五十五年前，那时我们都住在纽约州法罗克威的童年时代。理查德认识艾莉克斯大约二十二年了，她一直希望他能解释一下小粒子物理学，以便她和其他非物理学家也能理解。

I am delighted that Richard Feynman has agreed to give the first series of lectures. Our friendship goes back fifty-five years to our childhood in Far Rockaway, New York. Richard knew Alix for about twenty-two years, and she long sought to have him develop an explanation of the physics of small particles that would be understandable to her and to other non-physicists.

此外，我还要感谢那些为 Alix G. Mautner 基金捐款的人，正是他们的捐助才使得这些讲座成为可能。

As an added note, I would like to express my appreciation to those who contributed to the Alix G. Mautner Fund and thus helped make these lectures possible.

伦纳德·莫特纳​

LEONARD MAUTNER

加利福尼亚州洛杉矶

Los Angeles, California

1983年5月

May 1983

前言

Preface

理查德·费曼在物理学界享有盛名，因为他看待世界的方式：他从不认为任何事情是理所当然的，总是独立思考，因此他常常对自然界的行为有新的、深刻的理解——并且以一种清新、简洁优雅的方式进行描述。

Richard Feynman is legendary in the world of physics for the way he looks at the world: taking nothing for granted and always thinking things out for himself, he often attains a new and profound understanding of nature’s behavior—with a refreshing and elegantly simple way to describe it.

他还以热情地向学生讲解物理学而闻名。尽管拒绝了无数次在著名学会和组织发表演讲的邀请，费曼却对那些到他办公室请求他为当地高中物理俱乐部演讲的学生毫无抵抗力。

He is also known for his enthusiasm in explaining physics to students. After turning down countless offers to give speeches at prestigious societies and organizations, Feynman is a sucker for the student who comes by his office and asks him to talk to the local high school physics club.

据我们所知，这本书是一次前所未有的尝试。它以简洁明了、诚实易懂的方式，向非专业读者解释了量子电动力学理论这一相当深奥的主题。本书旨在帮助感兴趣的读者理解物理学家为了解释自然界的运行规律而采用的思维方式。

This book is a venture that, as far as we know, has never been tried. It is a straightforward, honest explanation of a rather difficult subject—the theory of quantum electrodynamics—for a nontechnical audience. It is designed to give the interested reader an appreciation for the kind of thinking that physicists have resorted to in order to explain how Nature behaves.

如果你正计划学习物理（或者已经在学习），这本书里没有任何需要“摒弃”的知识：它完整、准确地描述了一个框架，更高级的概念可以毫不费力地叠加在这个框架之上。对于那些已经学过物理的人来说，这本书会让你明白，当初那些复杂的计算究竟是怎么回事！

If you are planning to study physics (or are already doing so), there is nothing in this book that has to be “unlearned”: it is a complete description, accurate in every detail, of a framework onto which more advanced concepts can be attached without modification. For those of you who have already studied physics, it is a revelation of what you were really doing when you were making all those complicated calculations!

理查德·费曼小时候受到一本书的启发，开始学习微积分。这本书的开头是：“一个傻瓜能做到的事，另一个傻瓜也能做到。”他想用类似的话将这本书献给他的读者：“一个傻瓜能理解的，另一个傻瓜也能理解。”

As a boy, Richard Feynman was inspired to study calculus from a book that began, “What one fool can do, another can.” He would like to dedicate this book to his readers with similar words: “What one fool can understand, another can.”

R ·阿尔夫· L·艾顿

RALPH LEIGHTON

加利福尼亚州帕萨迪纳

Pasadena, California

1985年2月

February 1985

致谢

Acknowledgment

本书据称是我在加州大学洛杉矶分校讲授量子电动力学的记录，由我的好友拉尔夫·莱顿整理编辑而成。实际上，手稿经过了相当大的修改。莱顿先生在教学和写作方面的经验，对于我向更广泛的读者群体介绍物理学的这一核心部分起到了至关重要的作用。

This book purports to be a record of the lectures on quantum electrodynamics I gave at UCLA, transcribed and edited by my good friend Ralph Leighton. Actually, the manuscript has undergone considerable modification. Mr. Leighton’s experience in teaching and in writing was of considerable value in this attempt at presenting this central part of physics to a wider audience.

许多科普读物为了追求表面上的简洁，往往歪曲事实，描述与它们声称要描述的内容大相径庭。出于对学科的尊重，我们不能这样做。经过数小时的讨论，我们力求在不歪曲事实的前提下，做到最大程度的清晰简洁。

Many “popular” expositions of science achieve apparent simplicity only by describing something different, something considerably distorted from what they claim to be describing. Respect for our subject did not permit us to do this. Through many hours of discussion, we have tried to achieve maximum clarity and simplicity without compromise by distortion of the truth.

QED

QED

1

1

介绍

Introduction

艾莉克斯·莫特纳对物理非常好奇，经常让我给她讲解一些知识。我通常都能讲得不错，就像我每周四给加州理工学院的一群学生讲课一样，但最终，在我觉得最有趣的部分，我却总是讲不好：我们总是会被量子力学那些奇特的概念难住。我告诉她，我不可能在一个小时或一个晚上就解释清楚这些概念——这需要很长时间——但我向她承诺，总有一天我会准备一套关于这个主题的讲义。

Alix Mautner was very curious about physics and often asked me to explain things to her. I would do all right, just as I do with a group of students at Caltech that come to me for an hour on Thursdays, but eventually I’d fail at what is to me the most interesting part: We would always get hung up on the crazy ideas of quantum mechanics. I told her I couldn’t explain these ideas in an hour or an evening—it would take a long time—but I promised her that someday I’d prepare a set of lectures on the subject.

我准备了一些讲稿，然后去了趟新西兰试讲——因为新西兰离得够远，就算效果不好也没关系！结果新西兰人觉得还不错，所以我想应该没问题——至少对新西兰人来说是这样！这些是我真正为艾莉克斯准备的讲稿，可惜现在我不能直接跟她说。

I prepared some lectures, and I went to New Zealand to try them out—because New Zealand is far enough away that if they weren’t successful, it would be all right! Well, the people in New Zealand thought they were okay, so I guess they’re okay—at least for New Zealand! So here are the lectures I really prepared for Alix, but unfortunately I can’t tell them to her directly, now.

我想谈的是物理学中已知的部分，而不是未知的部分。人们总是追问各种理论统一的最新进展，却不给我们机会去介绍那些我们已经相当了解的理论。他们总是想知道我们不知道的事情。所以，与其用一堆不成熟、分析不充分的理论来迷惑你们，我更想和你们谈谈一个已经被非常透彻地分析过的主题。我非常喜欢物理学的这个领域，我认为它很精彩：它被称为量子电动力学，简称 QED。

What I’d like to talk about is a part of physics that is known, rather than a part that is unknown. People are always asking for the latest developments in the unification of this theory with that theory, and they don’t give us a chance to tell them anything about one of the theories that we know pretty well. They always want to know things that we don’t know. So, rather than confound you with a lot of half-cooked, partially analyzed theories, I would like to tell you about a subject that has been very thoroughly analyzed. I love this area of physics and I think it’s wonderful: it is called quantum electrodynamics, or QED for short.

我这次讲座的主要目的是尽可能准确地阐述光与物质的奇异理论——更确切地说，是光与电子的相互作用。要解释清楚所有我想讲的内容，需要很长时间。不过，一共四节课，所以我不会着急，我们会把所有问题都弄清楚的。

My main purpose in these lectures is to describe as accurately as I can the strange theory of light and matter—or more specifically, the interaction of light and electrons. It’s going to take a long time to explain all the things I want to. However, there are four lectures, so I’m going to take my time, and we will get everything all right.

物理学历来擅长将众多现象综合成少数几个理论。例如，早期物理学研究过运动现象、热现象、声音现象、光现象和引力现象。但随着艾萨克·牛顿爵士阐明运动定律，人们很快发现，这些看似不同的现象实际上是同一事物的不同方面。例如，声音现象可以完全理解为空气中原子的运动。因此，声音不再被视为运动之外的额外现象。人们还发现，热现象可以很容易地用运动定律来解释。就这样，大量的物理理论被综合成一个简化的理论。然而，引力理论却无法用运动定律来解释，即使在今天，它仍然与其他理论相分离。迄今为止，引力仍然无法用其他现象来解释。

Physics has a history of synthesizing many phenomena into a few theories. For instance, in the early days there were phenomena of motion and phenomena of heat; there were phenomena of sound, of light, and of gravity. But it was soon discovered, after Sir Isaac Newton explained the laws of motion, that some of these apparently different things were aspects of the same thing. For example, the phenomena of sound could be completely understood as the motion of atoms in the air. So sound was no longer considered something in addition to motion. It was also discovered that heat phenomena are easily understandable from the laws of motion. In this way, great globs of physics theory were synthesized into a simplified theory. The theory of gravitation, on the other hand, was not understandable from the laws of motion, and even today it stands isolated from the other theories. Gravitation is, so far, not understandable in terms of other phenomena.

在运动、声音和热现象被综合之后，人们又发现了我们今天称之为电磁现象的一系列现象。1873年，詹姆斯·克拉克·麦克斯韦将这些现象与光学现象综合起来，提出了一个统一的理论，他指出光是一种电磁波。因此，在那个阶段，人们已经掌握了运动定律、电磁定律和万有引力定律。

After the synthesis of the phenomena of motion, sound, and heat, there was the discovery of a number of phenomena that we call electrical and magnetic. In 1873 these phenomena were synthesized with the phenomena of light and optics into a single theory by James clerk Maxwell, who proposed that light is an electromagnetic wave. So at that stage, there were the laws of motion, the laws of electricity and magnetism, and the laws of gravity.

大约在1900年，人们发展出一种理论来解释物质的本质。它被称为物质的电子理论。它指出原子内部存在带电粒子。这一理论逐渐发展，最终包含了原子核及其周围电子运动的模型。

Around 1900 a theory was developed to explain what matter was. It was called the electron theory of matter, and it said that there were little charged particles inside of atoms. This theory evolved gradually to include a heavy nucleus with electrons going around it.

试图用力学定律来理解电子绕原子核运动——类似于牛顿用运动定律解释地球绕太阳公转的方式——却彻底失败了：各种预测都落空了。（顺便一提，你们都知道相对论是物理学的一项伟大革命，它也大约在同一时期发展起来。但与牛顿运动定律在原子层面完全失效的发现相比，相对论只不过是对其的一次微小修正。）建立一套新的体系来取代牛顿定律耗费了很长时间，因为原子层面的现象非常奇特。人们必须摒弃常识才能理解原子层面正在发生的事情。最终，在1926年，一种“非同寻常”的理论被提出，用来解释物质中电子的“新型行为”。它看起来荒谬可笑，但实际上并非如此：它被称为量子力学理论。 “量子”一词指的是自然界中这种违背常理的奇特现象。接下来我要讲的就是这个现象。

Attempts to understand the motion of the electrons going around the nucleus by using mechanical laws—analogous to the way Newton used the laws of motion to figure out how the earth went around the sun—were a real failure: all kinds of predictions came out wrong. (Incidentally, the theory of relativity, which you all understand to be a great revolution in physics, was also developed at about that time. But compared to this discovery that Newton’s laws of motion were quite wrong in atoms, the theory of relativity was only a minor modification.) Working out another system to replace Newton’s laws took a long time because phenomena at the atomic level were quite strange. One had to lose one’s common sense in order to perceive what was happening at the atomic level. Finally, in 1926, an “uncommon-sensy” theory was developed to explain the “new type of behavior” of electrons in matter. It looked cockeyed, but in reality it was not: it was called the theory of quantum mechanics. The word “quantum” refers to this peculiar aspect of nature that goes against common sense. It is this aspect that I am going to tell you about.

量子力学理论也解释了各种细节，例如氧原子为何与两个氢原子结合形成水等等。因此，量子力学为化学提供了理论基础。所以，基础理论化学实际上就是物理学。

The theory of quantum mechanics also explained all kinds of details, such as why an oxygen atom combines with two hydrogen atoms to make water, and so on. Quantum mechanics thus supplied the theory behind chemistry. So, fundamental theoretical chemistry is really physics.

由于量子力学理论能够解释所有的化学现象以及物质的各种性质，因此它取得了巨大的成功。但光与物质相互作用的问题仍然存在。也就是说，麦克斯韦的电磁理论必须进行修正，以符合新发展的量子力学原理。于是，一种新的理论——量子力学——应运而生。光与物质相互作用的理论，被赋予了一个可怕的名字——“量子电动力学”，最终由一些物理学家于 1929 年发展起来。

Because the theory of quantum mechanics could explain all of chemistry and the various properties of substances, it was a tremendous success. But still there was the problem of the interaction of light and matter. That is, Maxwell’s theory of electricity and magnetism had to be changed to be in accord with the new principles of quantum mechanics that had been developed. So a new theory, the quantum theory of the interaction of light and matter, which is called by the horrible name “quantum electrodynamics,” was finally developed by a number of physicists in 1929.

但这个理论存在缺陷。如果粗略计算，它会给出合理的结果。但如果尝试更精确地计算，你会发现原本以为很小的修正项（例如级数中的下一项）实际上非常大——实际上，它是无穷大！所以，事实证明，你无法真正计算出超过某个精度范围的任何内容。

But the theory was troubled. If you calculated something roughly, it would give a reasonable answer. But if you tried to compute it more accurately, you would find that the correction you thought was going to be small (the next term in a series, for example) was in fact very large—in fact, it was infinity! So it turned out you couldn’t really compute anything beyond a certain accuracy.

顺便说一句，我刚才概述的是我所谓的“物理学家眼中的物理学史”，这从来都不正确。我所说的是一种约定俗成的神话故事，物理学家们讲给他们的学生，而这些学生又讲给他们的学生，这未必与实际的历史发展有关，而我其实并不了解实际的历史发展！

By the way, what I have just outlined is what I call a “physicist’s history of physics,” which is never correct. What I am telling you is a sort of conventionalized myth-story that the physicists tell to their students, and those students tell to their students, and is not necessarily related to the actual historical development, which I do not really know!

总之，为了继续这段“历史”，保罗·狄拉克运用相对论，建立了一个相对论性的电子理论，但这个理论并没有完全考虑到电子与光相互作用的所有效应。狄拉克的理论认为，电子具有磁矩——类似于小磁铁的磁力——其强度在某些单位下恰好为1。大约在1948年，实验发现实际值更接近1.00118（最后一位数字的不确定度约为3）。当然，人们知道电子会与光相互作用，因此预期会有一些微小的修正。人们也预期这种修正可以从新的量子电动力学理论中得到解释。但是，当计算结果出现时，得到的不是1.00118，而是无穷大——这与实验结果不符！

At any rate, to continue with this “history,” Paul Dirac, using the theory of relativity, made a relativistic theory of the electron that did not completely take into account all the effects of the electron’s interaction with light. Dirac’s theory said that an electron had a magnetic moment—something like the force of a little magnet—that had a strength of exactly 1 in certain units. Then in about 1948 it was discovered in experiments that the actual number was closer to 1.00118 (with an uncertainty of about 3 on the last digit). It was known, of course, that electrons interact with light, so some small correction was expected. It was also expected that this correction would be understandable from the new theory of quantum electrodynamics. But when it was calculated, instead of 1.00118 the result was infinity—which is wrong, experimentally!

嗯，量子电动力学中如何计算这个问题，大约在1948年由朱利安·施温格、朝永慎一郎和我解决了。施温格是第一个使用新的“壳层”计算出这种修正的人。游戏”；他的理论值约为 1.00116，与实验值非常接近，表明我们的方向是正确的。最终，我们拥有了可以用来进行计算的电磁量子理论！这就是我接下来要向你们描述的理论。

Well, this problem of how to calculate things in quantum electrodynamics was straightened out by Julian Schwinger, Sin-Itiro Tomonaga, and myself in about 1948. Schwinger was the first to calculate this correction using a new “shell game”; his theoretical value was around 1.00116, which was close enough to the experimental number to show that we were on the right track. At last, we had a quantum theory of electricity and magnetism with which we could calculate! This is the theory that I am going to describe to you.

量子电动力学理论至今已发展超过五十年，并在越来越广泛的条件下得到了越来越精确的检验。目前我可以自豪地说，实验和理论之间没有显著差异！

The theory of quantum electrodynamics has now lasted for more than fifty years, and has been tested more and more accurately over a wider and wider range of conditions. At the present time I can proudly say that there is no significant difference between experiment and theory!

为了让您了解该理论经受了怎样的考验，我举几个最新的数据：实验测得狄拉克数为 1.00115965221（最后一位小数的误差约为 4）；理论计算结果为 1.00115965246（误差约为实验值的五倍）。为了让您体会这些数字的精确度，我们可以这样理解：如果用这种精度测量洛杉矶到纽约的距离，结果将精确到一根头发丝的粗细。这就是过去五十年来量子电动力学在理论和实验上所受到的检验的精细程度。顺便一提，我只举了一个例子。量子电动力学中还有其他一些量也以类似的精度进行了测量，结果也非常吻合。这些量级的尺度从地球大小的一百倍到原子核大小的百分之一，涵盖了各种距离尺度。这些数字旨在让你感到震惊，从而相信这个理论可能并非完全错误！在结束之前，我将解释这些计算是如何进行的。

Just to give you an idea of how the theory has been put through the wringer, I’ll give you some recent numbers: experiments have Dirac’s number at 1.00115965221 (with an uncertainty of about 4 in the last digit); the theory puts it at 1.00115965246 (with an uncertainty of about five times as much). To give you a feeling for the accuracy of these numbers, it comes out something like this: If you were to measure the distance from Los Angeles to New York to this accuracy, it would be exact to the thickness of a human hair. That’s how delicately quantum electrodynamics has, in the past fifty years, been checked—both theoretically and experimentally. By the way, I have chosen only one number to show you. There are other things in quantum electrodynamics that have been measured with comparable accuracy, which also agree very well. Things have been checked at distance scales that range from one hundred times the size of the earth down to one-hundredth the size of an atomic nucleus. These numbers are meant to intimidate you into believing that the theory is probably not too far off! Before we’re through, I’ll describe how these calculations are made.

我想再次向各位展示量子电动力学理论所描述的现象之广泛：反过来说更容易些：该理论描述了……除了引力效应（也就是让你坐在座位上的东西，实际上，我认为这是引力和礼貌的结合）和放射性现象（涉及原子核能级的跃迁）之外，物理世界的所有现象都与化学有关。所以，如果我们排除引力和放射性（更准确地说，是核物理），我们还剩下什么呢？汽车里的汽油燃烧、泡沫和气泡、盐或铜的硬度、钢的刚度。事实上，生物学家正试图用化学的视角来解释尽可能多的生命现象，而正如我之前解释过的，化学背后的理论是量子电动力学。

I would like to again impress you with the vast range of phenomena that the theory of quantum electrodynamics describes: It’s easier to say it backwards: the theory describes all the phenomena of the physical world except the gravitational effect, the thing that holds you in your seats (actually, that’s a combination of gravity and politeness, I think), and radioactive phenomena, which involve nuclei shifting in their energy levels. So if we leave out gravity and radioactivity (more properly, nuclear physics), what have we got left? Gasoline burning in automobiles, foam and bubbles, the hardness of salt or copper, the stiffness of steel. In fact, biologists are trying to interpret as much as they can about life in terms of chemistry, and as I already explained, the theory behind chemistry is quantum electrodynamics.

我必须澄清一点：当我说物理世界的所有现象都可以用这个理论解释时，我们其实并不确定。我们所熟悉的大多数现象都涉及数量庞大的电子，这种复杂性超出了我们有限的认知能力。在这种情况下，我们可以利用这个理论大致推断出应该发生什么，而这在当时的条件下也大致符合预期。但是，如果我们在实验室里设计一个只涉及少量电子、条件简单的实验，那么我们就可以非常精确地计算出可能发生的事情，也可以非常精确地测量它。每当我们进行这类实验时，量子电动力学理论都能很好地发挥作用。

I must clarify something: When I say that all the phenomena of the physical world can be explained by this theory, we don’t really know that. Most phenomena we are familiar with involve such tremendous numbers of electrons that it’s hard for our poor minds to follow that complexity. In such situations, we can use the theory to figure roughly what ought to happen and that is what happens, roughly, in those circumstances. But if we arrange in the laboratory an experiment involving just a few electrons in simple circumstances, then we can calculate what might happen very accurately, and we can measure it very accurately, too. Whenever we do such experiments, the theory of quantum electrodynamics works very well.

我们物理学家总是在不断检验理论是否存在问题。这就是乐趣所在，因为如果真的存在问题，那就太有趣了！但到目前为止，我们还没有发现量子电动力学理论存在任何问题。因此，我认为它是物理学的瑰宝——我们最引以为傲的成果。

We physicists are always checking to see if there is something the matter with the theory. That’s the game, because if there is something the matter, it’s interesting! But so far, we have found nothing wrong with the theory of quantum electrodynamics. It is, therefore, I would say, the jewel of physics—our proudest possession.

量子电动力学理论也是解释核现象（即原子核内部发生的各种活动）的新理论的原型。如果把物理世界想象成一个舞台，那么，这些“演员”不仅包括原子核外的电子，还包括原子核内的夸克、胶子等等——几十种不同的粒子。尽管这些“演员”看起来各不相同，但它们都以一种奇特的方式——一种“量子”方式——进行运动。最后，我会简单介绍一下原子核粒子。现在，为了简单起见，我只讲讲光子（光的粒子）和电子。因为重要的是它们运动的方式，而它们运动的方式非常有趣。

The theory of quantum electrodynamics is also the prototype for new theories that attempt to explain nuclear phenomena, the things that go on inside the nuclei of atoms. If one were to think of the physical world as a stage, then the actors would be not only electrons, which are outside the nucleus in atoms, but also quarks and gluons and so forth—dozens of kinds of particles—inside the nucleus. And though these “actors” appear quite different from one another, they all act in a certain style—a strange and peculiar style—the “quantum” style. At the end, I’ll tell you a little bit about the nuclear particles. In the meantime, I’m only going to tell you about photons—particles of light—and electrons, to keep it simple. Because it’s the way they act that is important, and the way they act is very interesting.

现在你们知道我要讲什么了。下一个问题是，你们能听懂我讲的内容吗？每个来听科学讲座的人都知道他们不可能完全听懂，但也许讲师会系一条漂亮的彩色领带供人欣赏。但费曼可没系领带！

So now you know what I’m going to talk about. The next question is, will you understand what I’m going to tell you? Everybody who comes to a scientific lecture knows they are not going to understand it, but maybe the lecturer has a nice, colored tie to look at. Not in this case! (Feynman is not wearing a tie.)

我要讲的内容，是我们研究生三四年级物理课上教的东西——你以为我会解释清楚让你听懂吗？不，你肯定听不懂。那我为什么要费这么大劲儿跟你讲这些？你又何必坐在这里听我讲呢？我的目的就是说服你不要因为听不懂就走开。你看，我的物理学生也听不懂。那是因为我听不懂。没人听得懂。

What I am going to tell you about is what we teach our physics students in the third or fourth year of graduate school—and you think I’m going to explain it to you so you can understand it? No, you’re not going to be able to understand it. Why, then, am I going to bother you with all this? Why are you going to sit here all this time, when you won’t be able to understand what I am going to say? It is my task to convince you not to turn away because you don’t understand it. You see, my physics students don’t understand it either. That is because I don’t understand it. Nobody does.

我想稍微谈谈理解的问题。听讲座时，你可能会因为很多原因听不懂演讲者的意思。其中一个原因是，他的语言表达能力不好——他要么没说出他想表达的意思，要么说得颠倒过来——导致难以理解。这只是个小问题，我会尽量避免过多地使用我的纽约口音。

I’d like to talk a little bit about understanding. When we have a lecture, there are many reasons why you might not understand the speaker. One is, his language is bad—he doesn’t say what he means to say, or he says it upside down—and it’s hard to understand. That’s a rather trivial matter, and I’ll try my best to avoid too much of my New York accent.

另一种可能性，尤其当讲师是物理学家时，是他以一种不寻常的方式使用普通词汇。物理学家经常出于某些技术目的使用“功”、“作用”、“能量”，甚至像你即将看到的“光”这样的普通词汇。因此，当我在物理学中谈论“功”时，我的意思与我在日常生活中谈论“工作”时的意思并不相同。在这次讲座中，我可能会在不知不觉中使用了这些词汇，而没有意识到它们的用法并不寻常。我会尽力避免犯错——这是我的职责——但这确实是一个很容易犯的错误。

Another possibility, especially if the lecturer is a physicist, is that he uses ordinary words in a funny way. Physicists often use ordinary words such as “work” or “action” or “energy” or even, as you shall see, “light” for some technical purpose. Thus, when I talk about “work” in physics, I don’t mean the same thing as when I talk about “work” on the street. During this lecture I might use one of those words without noticing that it is being used in this unusual way. I’ll try my best to catch myself—that’s my job—but it is an error that is easy to make.

你可能觉得听不懂我讲的下一个原因是，虽然我在描述自然界的运作方式，但你不会明白自然界为什么会这样运作。但你看，没有人能明白这一点。我也无法解释为什么自然界会以这种奇特的方式运行。

The next reason that you might think you do not understand what I am telling you is, while I am describing to you how Nature works, you won’t understand why Nature works that way. But you see, nobody understands that. I can’t explain why Nature behaves in this peculiar way.

最后，还有一种可能：我告诉你一些事情之后，你却无法相信，无法接受，甚至不喜欢。你仿佛被蒙上了一层心扉，不再听下去。我要向你描述自然的本来面目——如果你不喜欢，这就会阻碍你理解它。物理学家们已经学会了如何应对这个问题：他们逐渐意识到，喜欢或不喜欢某个理论并非关键所在。关键在于该理论的预测是否与实验结果相符。这与某个理论是否在哲学上令人愉悦、是否易于理解、或者是否完全符合常识无关。量子电动力学理论从常识的角度来看，将自然描述为荒谬的。而它与实验结果完全吻合。所以我希望你能接受自然本来的样子——荒谬。

Finally, there is this possibility: after I tell you something, you just can’t believe it. You can’t accept it. You don’t like it. A little screen comes down and you don’t listen anymore. I’m going to describe to you how Nature is—and if you don’t like it, that’s going to get in the way of your understanding it. It’s a problem that physicists have learned to deal with: They’ve learned to realize that whether they like a theory or they don’t like a theory is not the essential question. Rather, it is whether or not the theory gives predictions that agree with experiment. It is not a question of whether a theory is philosophically delightful, or easy to understand, or perfectly reasonable from the point of view of common sense. The theory of quantum electrodynamics describes Nature as absurd from the point of view of common sense. And it agrees fully with experiment. So I hope you can accept Nature as She is—absurd.

我会兴致勃勃地跟你讲讲这件荒诞的事，因为我觉得它妙趣横生。别因为觉得大自然如此奇特就放弃听我讲。请耐心听我说完，我希望讲完之后你也能和我一样感到欣喜。

I’m going to have fun telling you about this absurdity, because I find it delightful. Please don’t turn yourself off because you can’t believe Nature is so strange. Just hear me all out, and I hope you’ll be as delighted as I am when we’re through.

我该如何向你们解释那些我直到学生读研三年级才会讲解的内容呢？让我用类比的方式来解释。玛雅人对金星的升落很感兴趣，他们把金星当作晨星和昏星——他们非常想知道金星何时出现。经过多年的观察，他们发现金星的五个周期几乎等于他们“名义年”（365天）的八个周期（他们也知道实际的季节年有所不同，并对此进行了计算）。为了进行计算，玛雅人发明了一套用条形和点来表示数字（包括零）的系统，并制定了相应的规则，不仅可以计算和预测金星的升落，还可以预测其他天象，例如月食。

How am I going to explain to you the things I don’t explain to my students until they are third-year graduate students? Let me explain it by analogy. The Maya Indians were interested in the rising and setting of Venus as a morning “star” and as an evening “star”—they were very interested in when it would appear. After some years of observation, they noted that five cycles of Venus were very nearly equal to eight of their “nominal years” of 365 days (they were aware that the true year of seasons was different and they made calculations of that also). To make calculations, the Maya had invented a system of bars and dots to represent numbers (including zero), and had rules by which to calculate and predict not only the risings and settings of Venus, but other celestial phenomena, such as lunar eclipses.

在那个年代，只有少数玛雅祭司能够进行如此复杂的计算。现在，假设我们问其中一位祭司，如何计算预测金星何时再次作为晨星升起过程中的一个步骤——两个数字相减。再假设，不像今天这样，我们没有上过学，也不懂减法。那么，这位祭司会如何向我们解释什么是减法呢？

In those days, only a few Maya priests could do such elaborate calculations. Now, suppose we were to ask one of them how to do just one step in the process of predicting when Venus will next rise as a morning star—subtracting two numbers. And let’s assume that, unlike today, we had not gone to school and did not know how to subtract. How would the priest explain to us what subtraction is?

他可以教我们用竖线和圆点表示的数字以及“减法”的规则，也可以告诉我们他实际在做什么：“假设我们要从584中减去236。首先，数出584颗豆子，把它们放进锅里。然后取出236颗豆子放在一边。最后，数一数锅里剩下的豆子。这个数字就是从584中减去236的结果。”

He could either teach us the numbers represented by the bars and dots and the rules for “subtracting” them, or he could tell us what he was really doing: “Suppose we want to subtract 236 from 584. First, count out 584 beans and put them in a pot. Then take out 236 beans and put them to one side. Finally, count the beans left in the pot. That number is the result of subtracting 236 from 584.”

你可能会说：“我的天哪！数豆子、放豆子、放豆子、放豆子——真是个苦差事！”

You might say, “My Quetzalcoatl! What tedium—counting beans, putting them in, taking them out—what a job!”

对此，牧师会回答说：“这就是为什么我们要有关于横线和点的规则。这些规则虽然复杂，但比起数豆子，它们能更有效地得出答案。重要的是，就答案而言，规则并不重要：我们可以……”预测金星出现的方法有两种：一种是数豆子（虽然慢，但容易理解）；另一种是使用复杂的规则（虽然快得多，但你必须花数年时间在学校学习）。

To which the priest would reply, “That’s why we have the rules for the bars and dots. The rules are tricky, but they are a much more efficient way of getting the answer than by counting beans. The important thing is, it makes no difference as far as the answer is concerned: we can predict the appearance of Venus by counting beans (which is slow, but easy to understand) or by using the tricky rules (which is much faster, but you must spend years in school to learn them).”

理解减法的原理——只要你不必实际操作——其实并不难。我的观点是：我会向你们解释物理学家在预测自然规律时所做的工作，但我不会教你们任何技巧让你们高效地完成这项工作。你们会发现，为了用这套新的量子电动力学体系做出任何合理的预测，你们需要在纸上画出无数个小箭头。训练我们的物理系学生用这种巧妙而高效的方式完成这项工作，需要七年时间——四年本科和三年研究生。而我们打算跳过这七年的物理教育：通过用我们实际操作的方式来解释量子电动力学，我希望你们能够比一些学生理解得更透彻！

To understand how subtraction works—as long as you don’t have to actually carry it out—is really not so difficult. That’s my position: I’m going to explain to you what the physicists are doing when they are predicting how Nature will behave, but I’m not going to teach you any tricks so you can do it efficiently. You will discover that in order to make any reasonable predictions with this new scheme of quantum electrodynamics, you would have to make an awful lot of little arrows on a piece of paper. It takes seven years—four undergraduate and three graduate—to train our physics students to do that in a tricky, efficient way. That’s where we are going to skip seven years of education in physics: By explaining quantum electrodynamics to you in terms of what we are really doing, I hope you will be able to understand it better than do some of the students!

以玛雅人为例，我们可以进一步探讨这个问题：为什么金星的五个周期接近2920天，也就是八年？人们可能会提出各种各样的解释，比如“20在我们计数系统中是一个重要的数字，2920除以20等于146，而146比可以用两种不同方式表示的两个平方数之和的数值大1”，等等。但这些理论实际上与金星本身并无关联。在现代，我们发现这类理论毫无用处。所以，我们不会去探究自然界为何会以如此奇特的方式运行；因为目前还没有好的理论能够解释这种现象。

Taking the example of the Maya one step further, we could ask the priest why five cycles of Venus nearly equal 2,920 days, or eight years. There would be all kinds of theories about why, such as, “20 is an important number in our counting system, and if you divide 2,920 by 20, you get 146, which is one more than a number that can be represented by the sum of two squares in two different ways,” and so forth. But that theory would have nothing to do with Venus, really. In modern times, we have found that theories of this kind are not useful. So again, we are not going to deal with why Nature behaves in the peculiar way that She does; there are no good theories to explain that.

我目前所做的，就是让你进入合适的状态，听我说话。否则，我们一点机会都没有。好了，现在我们出发吧！

What I have done so far is to get you into the right mood to listen to me. Otherwise, we have no chance. So now we’re off, ready to go!

我们从光说起。牛顿开始研究光时，首先发现的是白光是由多种颜色混合而成的。他用棱镜将白光分解成各种颜色，但当他将一种颜色的光——例如红色——通过另一个棱镜时，却发现它无法被进一步分离。因此，牛顿得出结论：白光是由多种不同颜色混合而成的，每一种颜色都是“纯”的，因为它们无法被进一步分离。

We begin with light. When Newton started looking at light, the first thing he found was that white light is a mixture of colors. He separated white light with a prism into various colors, but when he put light of one color—red, for instance—through another prism, he found it could not be separated further. So Newton found that white light is a mixture of different colors, each of which is pure in the sense that it can’t be separated further.

（事实上​​，特定颜色的光还可以根据其所谓的“偏振”以另一种方式再次分裂。光的这一特性对于理解量子电动力学的本质并非至关重要，因此为了简便起见，我将省略它——但这会导致我无法提供该理论的完整描述。这种轻微的简化不会以任何方式妨碍您对我要讨论的内容的真正理解。尽管如此，我仍需谨慎地提及所有我省略的内容。）

(In fact, a particular color of light can be split one more time in a different way, according to its so-called “polarization.” This aspect of light is not vital to understanding the character of quantum electrodynamics, so for the sake of simplicity I will leave it out—at the expense of not giving you an absolutely complete description of the theory. This slight simplification will not remove, in any way, any real understanding of what I will be talking about. Still, I must be careful to mention all of the things I leave out.)

在这些讲座中，我所说的“光”并非仅仅指我们肉眼可见的红光、蓝光。事实上，可见光只是一个漫长光谱的一部分，它类似于音阶，其中包含着人耳听不到的音符和人耳听不到的音符。光的光谱可以用数字来描述——称为频率——随着频率的升高，光从红色变为蓝色，再变为紫色，最后变为紫外线。我们肉眼看不到紫外线，但它可以影响感光底片。它仍然是光——只是频率不同而已。（我们不应该如此局限：我们用肉眼直接感知到的东西，并非世间万物！）如果我们继续改变频率，就会得到X射线、伽马射线等等。如果我们反过来改变频率，就会从蓝色变为红色，再变为红外线（热波），然后是电视波和无线电波。对我来说，所有这些都是“光”。我的大部分例子都将使用红光，但量子电动力学理论它涵盖了我所描述的所有范围，并且是所有这些不同现象背后的理论。

When I say “light” in these lectures, I don’t mean simply the light we can see, from red to blue. It turns out that visible light is just a part of a long scale that’s analogous to a musical scale in which there are notes higher than you can hear and other notes lower than you can hear. The scale of light can be described by numbers—called the frequency—and as the numbers get higher, the light goes from red to blue to violet to ultraviolet. We can’t see ultraviolet light, but it can affect photographic plates. It’s still light—only the number is different. (We shouldn’t be so provincial: what we can detect directly with our own instrument, the eye, isn’t the only thing in the world!) If we continue simply to change the number, we go out into X-rays, gamma rays, and so on. If we change the number in the other direction, we go from blue to red to infrared (heat) waves, then television waves, and radio waves. For me, all of that is “light.” I’m going to use just red light for most of my examples, but the theory of quantum electrodynamics extends over the entire range I have described, and is the theory behind all these various phenomena.

牛顿认为光是由粒子组成的——他称之为“微粒”——他的观点是正确的（但他得出这个结论的推理是错误的）。我们知道光是由粒子组成的，因为我们可以使用一种非常灵敏的仪器，当光照射到它时会发出咔哒声；如果光线变暗，咔哒声仍然很大——只是数量减少了。因此，光就像雨滴一样——每一小团光都被称为光子——如果光都是同一种颜色，那么所有的“雨滴”大小都相同。

Newton thought that light was made up of particles—he called them “corpuscles”—and he was right (but the reasoning that he used to come to that decision was erroneous). We know that light is made of particles because we can take a very sensitive instrument that makes clicks when light shines on it, and if the light gets dimmer, the clicks remain just as loud—there are just fewer of them. Thus light is something like raindrops—each little lump of light is called a photon—and if the light is all one color, all the “raindrops” are the same size.

人眼是一个非常精密的仪器：只需要五六个光子就能激活一个神经细胞，并将信息传递到大脑。如果我们进化得更进一步，视力能提高十倍，我们就无需进行这样的讨论了——我们所有人都会看到非常微弱的单一颜色光线，它们会呈现为一系列强度相同的间歇性闪光。

The human eye is a very good instrument: it takes only about five or six photons to activate a nerve cell and send a message to the brain. If we were evolved a little further so we could see ten times more sensitively, we wouldn’t have to have this discussion—we would all have seen very dim light of one color as a series of intermittent little flashes of equal intensity.

你可能想知道如何探测单个光子。光电倍增管就是一种可以做到这一点的仪器，我来简要介绍一下它的工作原理：当一个光子撞击底部的金属板 A（见图1）时，它会使金属板中的一个原子脱离电子束缚。这个自由电子会被带正电荷的金属板 B 强烈吸引，并以足够的力撞击 B，从而使三到四个电子脱离束缚。每个从金属板 B 脱离的电子都会被带电的金属板 C 吸引，它们与金属板 C 的碰撞会进一步使更多的电子脱离束缚。这个过程重复十到十二次，直到数十亿个电子（足以产生相当大的电流）撞击到最后一个金属板 L。这个电流可以通过普通放大器放大，并通过扬声器发出可听见的咔嗒声。每次当特定颜色的光子撞击光电倍增管时，会听到音量均匀的咔嗒声。

You might wonder how it is possible to detect a single photon. One instrument that can do this is called a photomultiplier, and I’ll describe briefly how it works: When a photon hits the metal plate A at the bottom (see Figure 1), it causes an electron to break loose from one of the atoms in the plate. The free electron is strongly attracted to plate B (which has a positive charge on it) and hits it with enough force to break loose three or four electrons. Each of the electrons knocked out of plate B is attracted to plate C (which is also charged), and their collision with plate C knocks loose even more electrons. This process is repeated ten or twelve times, until billions of electrons, enough to make a sizable electric current, hit the last plate, L. This current can be amplified by a regular amplifier and sent through a speaker to make audible clicks. Each time a photon of a given color hits the photomultiplier, a click of uniform loudness is heard.

如果你在周围放置许多光电倍增管，并让一些非常微弱的光向各个方向照射，光线会进入其中一个或另一个光电倍增管，并发出全强度的咔嗒声。这是要么全有要么全无的现象：如果一个光电倍增管在某一时刻发出光，其他光电倍增管不会同时发出光（除非极少数情况下，两个光子恰好同时离开光源）。光不会分裂成各自前往不同方向的“半粒子”。

If you put a whole lot of photomultipliers around and let some very dim light shine in various directions, the light goes into one multiplier or another and makes a click of full intensity. It is all or nothing: if one photomultiplier goes off at a given moment, none of the others goes off at the same moment (except in the rare instance that two photons happened to leave the light source at the same time). There is no splitting of light into “half particles” that go different places.

图1.光电倍增管可以探测单个光子。当一个光子撞击极板 A 时，会击出一个电子并被吸引到带正电的极板 B 上，从而击出更多电子。这个过程持续进行，直到数十亿个电子撞击最后一个极板 L，产生电流，该电流由普通放大器放大。如果将扬声器连接到放大器，则每次特定颜色的光子撞击极板 A 时，都会听到音量均匀的咔嗒声。

FIGURE 1. A photomultiplier can detect a single photon. When a photon strikes plate A, an electron is knocked loose and attracted to positively charged plate B, knocking more electrons loose. This process continues until billions of electrons strike the last plate, L, and produce an electric current, which is amplified by a regular amplifier. If a speaker is connected to the amplifier, clicks of uniform loudness are heard each time a photon of a given color hits plate A.

我想强调的是，光是以粒子的形式存在的。了解光具有粒子性非常重要，尤其是对于那些上过学的人来说，你们可能学过光具有波动性。我现在要告诉你们的是，光实际上具有粒子性。

I want to emphasize that light comes in this form—particles. It is very important to know that light behaves like particles, especially for those of you who have gone to school, where you were probably told something about light behaving like waves. I’m telling you the way it does behave—like particles.

你可能会说，光电倍增管只是把光当作粒子来检测，但事实并非如此，所有被设计得足够灵敏以检测微弱光的仪器最终都发现了同一件事：光是由粒子组成的。

You might say that it’s just the photomultiplier that detects light as particles, but no, every instrument that has been designed to be sensitive enough to detect weak light has always ended up discovering the same thing: light is made of particles.

我假设你们都熟悉光在日常生活中的一些特性——比如，光沿直线传播；光入射到水中会发生弯曲；当光从镜子等表面反射时，入射角等于出射角；光可以被分解成不同的颜色；当泥坑里沾上一点油时，就能看到美丽的色彩；透镜可以聚焦光线，等等。我将利用这些你们熟悉的现象来阐释光的奇特行为；我将用量子电动力学理论来解释这些熟悉的现象。我之前提到光电倍增管是为了说明一个你们可能不太了解的基本现象——光是由粒子构成的——但现在，我希望你们也已经了解了这一点！

I am going to assume that you are familiar with the properties of light in everyday circumstances—things like, light goes in straight lines; it bends when it goes into water; when it is reflected from a surface like a mirror, the angle at which the light hits the surface is equal to the angle at which it leaves the surface; light can be separated into colors; you can see beautiful colors on a mud puddle when there is a little bit of oil on it; a lens focuses light, and so on. I am going to use these phenomena that you are familiar with in order to illustrate the truly strange behavior of light; I am going to explain these familiar phenomena in terms of the theory of quantum electrodynamics. I told you about the photomultiplier in order to illustrate an essential phenomenon that you may not have been familiar with—that light is made of particles—but by now, I hope you are familiar with that, too!

我想大家都熟悉光线会被某些表面（例如水面）部分反射的现象。许多浪漫画作都描绘了月光洒在湖面上的景象（当然，你们也肯定因为湖面反射的月光而惹上过麻烦！）。当你向下看水面时，你可以看到水面下的景象（尤其是在白天），同时也能看到水面的反射。玻璃是另一个例子：如果你在房间里开着灯，白天透过窗户向外看，你不仅能透过玻璃看到外面的景象，还能看到房间里灯光的微弱反射。所以，光线会被玻璃表面部分反射。

Now, I think you are all familiar with the phenomenon that light is partly reflected from some surfaces, such as water. Many are the romantic paintings of moonlight reflecting from a lake (and many are the times you got yourself in trouble because of moonlight reflecting from a lake!). When you look down into water you can see what’s below the surface (especially in the daytime), but you can also see a reflection from the surface. Glass is another example: if you have a lamp on in the room and you’re looking out through a window during the daytime, you can see things outside through the glass as well as a dim reflection of the lamp in the room. So light is partially reflected from the surface of glass.

在继续之前，我想先说明一下我即将做的一个简化，稍后我会进行修正：当我谈到玻璃对光的局部反射时，我会假设光只被玻璃表面反射。但实际上，一块玻璃的结构极其复杂——大量的电子在其中剧烈运动。当光子入射时，它会与玻璃内部的电子相互作用，而不仅仅是玻璃表面的电子。光子和电子会进行某种形式的相互作用，其最终结果与光子仅撞击玻璃表面时的结果相同。所以，我先做个简化的解释。稍后，我会向你展示玻璃内部实际发生的情况，这样你就能理解为什么结果相同了。

Before I go on, I want you to be aware of a simplification I am going to make that I will correct later on: When I talk about the partial reflection of light by glass, I am going to pretend that the light is reflected by only the surface of the glass. In reality, a piece of glass is a terrible monster of complexity—huge numbers of electrons are jiggling about. When a photon comes down, it interacts with electrons throughout the glass, not just on the surface. The photon and electrons do some kind of dance, the net result of which is the same as if the photon hit only the surface. So let me make that simplification for a while. Later on, I’ll show you what actually happens inside the glass so you can understand why the result is the same.

现在我想描述一个实验，并告诉大家它令人惊讶的结果。在这个实验中，一些相同颜色的光子——比如说，红光——从光源（见图2）向下照射到一块玻璃上。光电倍增管放置在玻璃上方的A点，用来接收被玻璃表面反射的光子。为了测量有多少光子穿过玻璃表面，另一个光电倍增管放置在玻璃内部的B点。先不说把光电倍增管放在玻璃内部的明显困难；这个实验的结果是什么呢？

Now I’d like to describe an experiment, and tell you its surprising results. In this experiment some photons of the same color—let’s say, red light—are emitted from a light source (see Fig. 2) down toward a block of glass. A photomultiplier is placed at A, above the glass, to catch any photons that are reflected by the front surface. To measure how many photons get past the front surface, another photomultiplier is placed at B, inside the glass. Never mind the obvious difficulties of putting a photomultiplier inside a block of glass; what are the results of this experiment?

图2.测量单层玻璃表面对光的部分反射的实验。每 100 个从光源发出的光子中，有4 个被前表面反射并最终到达 A 处的光电倍增管，而其余 96 个被前表面透射并最终到达 B 处的光电倍增管。

FIGURE 2. An experiment to measure the partial reflection of light by a single surface of glass. For every 100 photons that leave the light source, 4 are reflected by the front surface and end up in the photomultiplier at A, while the other 96 are transmitted by the front surface and end up in the photomultiplier at B.

每100个以90°角垂直射向玻璃的光子中，平均有4个到达A点，96个到达B点。因此，这里的“部分反射”指的是4%的光子被玻璃的前表面反射，而其余96%则透射过去。这就带来了一个难题：光怎么可能发生部分反射呢？每个光子最终都会到达B点。光子究竟应该飞向A点还是B点？它是如何“决定”飞向A点还是B点的呢？（观众大笑）这听起来像个笑话，但我们不能只是笑一笑；我们必须用理论来解释这个问题！部分反射本身就是一个深奥的谜题，即使是牛顿也曾为此苦苦思索。

For every 100 photons that go straight down toward the glass at 90°, an average of 4 arrive at A and 96 arrive at B. So “partial reflection” in this case means that 4% of the photons are reflected by the front surface of the glass, while the other 96% are transmitted. Already we are in great difficulty: how can light be partly reflected? Each photon ends up at A or B—how does the photon “make up its mind” whether it should go to A or B? (Audience laughs.) That may sound like a joke, but we can’t just laugh; we’re going to have to explain that in terms of a theory! Partial reflection is already a deep mystery, and it was a very difficult problem for Newton.

关于玻璃的部分反射现象，人们可以提出几种可能的理论。其中一种理论认为，玻璃表面96%是允许光线穿过的“孔洞”，而剩余4%的表面则覆盖着微小的反射材料“斑点”（见图3）。牛顿意识到这种解释行不通。¹稍后我们将遇到部分反射的一个奇特现象，如果你坚持“孔洞和斑点”理论——或者任何其他合理的理论——它都会让你抓狂！

There are several possible theories that you could make up to account for the partial reflection of light by glass. One of them is that 96% of the surface of the glass is “holes” that let the light through, while the other 4% of the surface is covered by small “spots” of reflective material (see Fig. 3). Newton realized that this is not a possible explanation.1 In just a moment we will encounter a strange feature of partial reflection that will drive you crazy if you try to stick to a theory of “holes and spots”—or to any other reasonable theory!

另一种可能的理论是，光子内部存在某种机制——内部有“齿轮”和“轮子”在以某种方式运转——当光子“瞄准”得当时，它就能穿过玻璃；而当它瞄准不当时，就会被反射。我们可以通过以下方法检验这个理论：在光源和第一层玻璃之间放置几层额外的玻璃，以此来过滤掉那些瞄准不当的光子。经过这些过滤层后，到达玻璃表面的光子应该全部瞄准得当，并且不会发生反射。然而，这个理论的问题在于，它与实验结果不符：即使经过多层玻璃，到达特定表面的光子中仍然有4%会发生反射。

Another possible theory is that the photons have some kind of internal mechanism—“wheels” and “gears” inside that are turning in some way—so that when a photon is “aimed” just right, it goes through the glass, and when it’s not aimed right, it reflects. We can check this theory by trying to filter out the photons that are not aimed right by putting a few extra layers of glass between the source and the first layer of glass. After going through the filters, the photons reaching the glass should all be aimed right, and none of them should reflect. The trouble with that theory is, it doesn’t agree with experiment: even after going through many layers of glass, 4% of the photons reaching a given surface reflect off it.

无论我们如何努力，试图构建一个合理的理论来解释光子如何“决定”是穿过玻璃还是被反射回来，我们都无法预测某个特定光子的运动方向。哲学家们曾说过，如果相同的条件不能总是产生相同的结果，那么预测就不可能，科学也将崩溃。然而，这里就存在这样一种情况：相同的光子总是以相同的方向射向同一块玻璃，却​​产生了不同的结果。我们无法预测某个特定光子会到达A点还是B点。我们唯一能预测的是，在100个入射光子中，平均有4个会被玻璃表面反射。这是否意味着，物理学这门高度精确的科学，如今只能计算事件发生的概率，而无法准确预测事件的发生？是的。这是一种退步，但事实就是如此：自然界只允许我们计算概率。然而，科学并没有崩溃。

Try as we might to invent a reasonable theory that can explain how a photon “makes up its mind” whether to go through glass or bounce back, it is impossible to predict which way a given photon will go. Philosophers have said that if the same circumstances don’t always produce the same results, predictions are impossible and science will collapse. Here is a circumstance—identical photons are always coming down in the same direction to the same piece of glass—that produces different results. We cannot predict whether a given photon will arrive at A or B. All we can predict is that out of 100 photons that come down, an average of 4 will be reflected by the front surface. Does this mean that physics, a science of great exactitude, has been reduced to calculating only the probability of an event, and not predicting exactly what will happen? Yes. That’s a retreat, but that’s the way it is: Nature permits us to calculate only probabilities. Yet science has not collapsed.

图3.解释单个表面部分反射的一种理论涉及一个主要由允许光线通过的“孔”组成的表面，以及一些反射光线的“点”。

FIGURE 3. One theory to explain partial reflection by a single surface involves a surface made up mainly of “holes” that let light through, with a few “spots” that reflect the light.

单表面的部分反射是一个深奥的谜题，也是一个难题，而两个或多个表面的部分反射则更是令人匪夷所思。让我来解释一下。接下来我们将解释原因。我们将进行第二个实验，测量两个表面对光的部分反射。我们将用一块非常薄的玻璃片代替玻璃块——这块玻璃片的两个表面完全平行——并将光电倍增管放置在玻璃片下方，与光源对齐。这次，光子可以从前表面或后表面反射到达 A 点；其余的光子将到达 B 点（见图4）。我们预计前表面会反射 4% 的光，后表面会反射剩余 96% 光中的 4%，总共约 8%。因此，我们应该发现，每 100 个离开光源的光子中，大约有 8 个到达 A 点。

While partial reflection by a single surface is a deep mystery and a difficult problem, partial reflection by two or more surfaces is absolutely mind-boggling. Let me show you why. We’ll do a second experiment, in which we will measure the partial reflection of light by two surfaces. We replace the block of glass with a very thin sheet of glass—its two surfaces are exactly parallel to each other—and we place the photomultiplier below the sheet of glass, in line with the light source. This time, photons can reflect from either the front surface or the back surface to end up at A; all the others will end up at B (see Fig. 4).
