In this example, the first amplitude has a length of 0.2 and is turned toward 2 o’clock. If we begin with a “unit arrow”—an arrow of length 1 pointed straight up—we can shrink this unit arrow from 1 down to 0.2, and turn it from 12 o’clock to 2 o’clock. The amplitude for the second step can be thought of as shrinking the unit arrow from 1 to 0.3 and turning it from 12 o’clock to 5 o’clock.

现在，为了合并这两个步骤的振幅，我们依次进行缩小和旋转操作。首先，我们将单位箭头从 1 缩小到 0.2，并将其从 12 点钟方向旋转到 2 点钟方向；然后，我们进一步缩小箭头，从 0.2 缩小到其十分之三，并将其旋转 12 到 5 的距离——也就是说，将其从 2 点钟方向旋转到 7 点钟方向。最终得到的箭头长度为 0.06，指向 7 点钟方向。它代表概率为 0.06 的平方，即 0.0036。

Now, to combine the amplitudes for both steps, we shrink and turn in succession. First, we shrink the unit arrow from 1 to 0.2 and turn it from 12 to 2 o’clock; then we shrink the arrow further, from 0.2 down to three-tenths of that, and turn it by the amount from 12 to 5—that is, we turn it from 2 o’clock to 7 o’clock. The resulting arrow has a length of 0.06 and is pointed toward 7 o’clock. It represents a probability of 0.06 squared, or 0.0036.

仔细观察箭头，我们发现，连续缩小并旋转两个箭头的结果，等于将它们的角度（2点钟方向 + 5点钟方向）相加，再将它们的长度（0.2 × 0.3）相乘。理解为什么要将角度相加也很容易：箭头的角度是由假想的秒表指针的转动量决定的。因此，连续两步的总转动量，就是第一步的转动量加上第二步的额外转动量。

Observing the arrows carefully, we see that the result of shrinking and turning two arrows in succession is the same as adding their angles (2 o’clock + 5 o’clock) and multiplying their lengths (0.2 * 0.3). To understand why we add the angles is easy: the angle of an arrow is determined by the amount of turning by the imaginary stopwatch hand. So the total amount of turning for the two steps in succession is simply the sum of the turning for the first step plus the additional turning for the second step.

我们为什么称这个过程为“乘箭头”需要一些解释，但这很有趣。让我们暂时从古希腊人的角度来看一下乘法（这与本次讲座无关）。古希腊人想要使用不一定是整数的数字，所以他们用线条来表示数字。任何数字都可以通过单位线的变换来表示——通过延长或缩短它。例如，如果线条 A 是……单位线（见图38），则线 B 代表 2，线 C 代表 3。

Why we call this process “multiplying arrows” takes a bit more explanation, but it’s interesting. Let’s look at multiplication, for a moment, from the point of view of the Greeks (this has nothing to do with the lecture). The Greeks wanted to use numbers that were not necessarily integers, so they represented numbers with lines. Any number can be expressed as a transformation of the unit line—by expanding it or shrinking it. For example, if Line A is the unit line (see Fig. 38), then line B represents 2 and line C represents 3.

那么，3 乘以 2 怎么算呢？我们依次进行变换：以线段 A 为单位线，先将其放大 2 倍，再放大 3 倍（或者先放大 3 倍再放大 2 倍——顺序无关紧要）。结果就是线段 D，它的长度代表 6。那么 1/3 乘以 1/2 呢？同样以线段 D 为单位线，我们先将其缩小到 1/2（线段 C），然后再缩小到 C 的 1/3。结果就是线段 A，它的长度代表 1/6。

Now, how do we multiply 3 times 2? We apply the transformations in succession: starting with line A as the unit line, we expand it 2 times and then 3 times (or 3 times and then 2 times—the order doesn’t make any difference). The result is line D, whose length represents 6. What about multiplying 1/3 times 1/2? Taking line D to be the unit line, now, we shrink it to 1/2 (line C) and then to 1/3 of that. The result is line A, which represents 1/6.

图38。我们可以将任何数字表示为单位线通过伸缩变换的结果。如果 A 是单位线，那么 B 代表 2（伸缩），C 代表 3（伸缩）。乘法运算是通过连续变换实现的。例如，3 乘以 2 意味着单位线先伸缩 3 倍，再伸缩 2 倍，结果为 6（线段 D）。如果 D 是单位线，那么线段 C 代表 112（伸缩），线段 B 代表 113（伸缩），112 乘以 113 意味着单位线 D 先伸缩到 112，再伸缩到 113 的 113 倍，结果为 1/6（线段 A）。

FIGURE 38. We can express any number as a transformation of the unit line through expansion or shrinkage. If A is the unit line, then B represents 2 (expansion), and C represents 3 (expansion). Multiplying lines is achieved through successive transformations. For example, multiplying 3 by 2 means that the unit line is expanded 3 times and then 2 times, producing the answer, an expansion of 6 (line D). If D is the unit line, then line C represents 112 (shrinkage), line B represents 113 (shrinkage), and multiplying 112 by 113 means the unit line D is shrunk to 112, and then to 113 of that, producing the answer, a shrinkage to 1/6 (line A).

箭头的乘法运算方法相同（见图39）。我们对单位箭头依次进行变换——恰好箭头的变换涉及两个操作：缩小和旋转。要将箭头 V 乘以箭头 W，我们先将单位箭头按 V 的指定量缩小并旋转，然后再按 W 的指定量缩小并旋转——同样，顺序无关紧要。因此，箭头的乘法运算遵循与普通数字相同的连续变换规则。4

Multiplying arrows works the same way (see Fig. 39). We apply transformations to the unit arrow in succession—it just happens that the transformation of an arrow involves two operations, a shrink and turn. To multiply arrow V times arrow W, we shrink and turn the unit arrow by the prescribed amounts for V, and then shrink it and turn it the amounts prescribed for W—again, the order doesn’t make any difference. So multiplying arrows follows the same rule of successive transformations that work for regular numbers.4

图39.数学家发现，箭头的乘法也可以表示为单位箭头的连续变换（就我们的目的而言，是连续的缩小和旋转）。与普通乘法一样，顺序并不重要：答案箭头 X 可以通过箭头 V 乘以箭头 W 或箭头 W 乘以箭头 V 得到。

FIGURE 39. Mathematicians found that multiplying arrows can also be expressed as successive transformations (for our purposes, successive shrinks and turns) of the unit arrow. As in normal multiplication, the order is not important: the answer, arrow X, can be obtained by multiplying arrow V by arrow W or arrow W by arrow V.

让我们回到第一讲的第一个实验——单表面部分反射——并牢记这个逐步分解的概念（见图40）。我们可以将反射路径分为三个步骤：1）光线从光源向下照射到玻璃表面；2）光线被玻璃表面反射；3）光线从玻璃表面向上照射到探测器。每个步骤都可以看作是单位箭头的一定程度的收缩和旋转。

Let’s go back to the first experiment from the first lecture—partial reflection by a single surface—with this idea of successive steps in mind (see Fig. 40). We can divide the path of reflection into three steps: 1) the light goes from the source down to the glass, 2) it is reflected by the glass, and 3) it goes from the glass up to the detector. Each step can be considered as a certain amount of shrinking and turning of the unit arrow.

图40.单个表面的反射可以分为三个步骤，每个步骤都伴随着单位箭头的收缩和/或旋转。最终结果是一个长度为 0.2 的箭头指向某个方向，与之前相同，但我们现在的分析方法更加详细。

FIGURE 40. Reflection by a single surface can be divided into three steps, each with a shrink andlor turn of the unit arrow. The net result, an arrow of length 0.2 pointed in some direction, is the same as before, but our method of analysis is more detailed now.

你们应该还记得，在第一讲中，我们没有考虑光线在玻璃上反射的所有方式，这需要画出并添加很多很多细小的箭头。为了避免这些细节，我假设光线会汇聚到玻璃表面的一个特定点——它不会扩散。实际上，当光线从一点传播到另一点时，它确实会扩散（除非被透镜阻挡），并且与之相关的单位箭头也会有所收缩。但是，目前我还是想坚持光线不扩散的简化观点，因此这种说法是合适的。忽略这种收缩。此外，由于光不会扩散，因此假设每个离开光源的光子最终都会到达 A 点或 B 点也是合理的。

You’ll remember that in the first lecture, we did not consider all of the ways the light could reflect off the glass, which requires drawing and adding lots and lots of little tiny arrows. In order to avoid all that detail, I gave the impression that the light goes down to a particular point on the surface of the glass—that it doesn’t spread out. When light goes from one point to another, it does, in reality, spread out (unless it’s fooled by a lens), and there is some shrinkage of the unit arrow associated with that. For the moment, however, I would like to stick to the simplified view that light does not spread out, and so it is appropriate to disregard this shrinkage. It is also appropriate to assume that since the light doesn’t spread out, every photon that leaves the source ends up at either A or B.

所以：第一步没有收缩，但有旋转——这相当于假想的秒表指针在计时光子从光源到达玻璃前表面时旋转的幅度。在这个例子中，第一步的箭头最终在某个角度（比如说，5点钟方向）达到长度1。

So: in the first step there is no shrinking, but there is turning—it corresponds to the amount of turning by the imaginary stopwatch hand as it times the photon going from the source to the front surface of the glass. In this example, the arrow for the first step ends up with a length of 1 at some angle—let’s say, 5 o’clock.

第二步是光子被玻璃反射。这里会发生显著的收缩——从 1 到 0.2——并且旋转半圈。（这些数字现在看起来似乎有些随意：它们取决于光是由玻璃还是其他材料反射。在第三讲中，我也会解释它们！）因此，第二步可以用振幅为 0.2、方向为 6 点钟（半圈）来表示。

The second step is the reflection of the photon by the glass. Here, there is a sizable shrink—from 1 to 0.2—and half a turn. (These numbers seem arbitrary now: they depend upon whether the light is reflected by glass or some other material. In the third lecture, I’ll explain them, too!) Thus the second step is represented by an amplitude of length 0.2 and a direction of 6 o’clock (half a turn).

最后一步是光子从玻璃到达探测器。这里，和第一步一样，没有距离缩短，但发生了转向——假设这一距离比第一步略短，箭头指向4点钟方向。

The last step is the photon going from the glass up to the detector. Here, as in the first step, there is no shrinking, but there is turning—let’s say this distance is slightly shorter than in step 1, and the arrow points toward 4 o’clock.

现在我们依次“乘法”箭头 1、2 和 3（角度相加，长度相乘）。这三个步骤——1）旋转，2）缩小并旋转半圈，3）旋转——的最终效果与第一讲相同：步骤 1 和 3 的旋转量（5 点钟方向加 4 点钟方向）与我们之前让秒表走完整个距离（9 点钟方向）时得到的旋转量相同；步骤 2 的额外半圈旋转使箭头指向与秒表指针相反的方向，与第一讲相同；步骤 2 中缩小到 0.2 后，箭头上的方格代表了单个表面观察到的 4% 部分反射率。

We now “multiply” arrows 1,2, and 3 in succession (add the angles, and multiply the lengths). The net effect of the three steps—1) turning, 2) a shrink and half a turn, and 3) turning—is the same as in the first lecture: the turning from steps 1 and 3—(5 o’clock plus 4 o’clock) is the same amount of turning that we got then when we let the stopwatch run for the whole distance (9 o’clock); the extra half turn from step 2 makes the arrow point in the direction opposite the stopwatch hand, as it did in the first lecture, and the shrinking to 0.2 in the second step leaves an arrow whose square represents the 4% partial reflection observed for a single surface.

在这个实验中，有一个我们在第一讲中没有探讨的问题：到达 B 的光子——那些被玻璃表面透射的光子——会怎么样呢？光子到达 B 的振幅长度必须接近 0.98，因为 0.98 * 0.98 = 0.9604，这非常接近 96%。该振幅也可以通过将其分解为若干步骤来分析（见图41）。

In this experiment, there is a question we didn’t look at in the first lecture: what about the photons that go to B—the ones that are transmitted by the surface of the glass? The amplitude for a photon to arrive at B must have a length near 0.98, since 0.98 * 0.98 = 0.9604, which is close enough to 96%. This amplitude can also be analyzed by breaking it down into steps (see Fig. 41).

第一步与到达 A 的路径相同——光子从光源向下到达玻璃——单位箭头指向 5 点钟方向。

The first step is the same as for the path to A—the photon goes from the light source down to the glass—the unit arrow is turned toward 5 o’clock.

第二步是光子穿过玻璃表面：透射过程中没有转向，只有一点点收缩——到 0.98。

The second step is the photon passing through the surface of the glass: there is no turning associated with transmission, just a little bit of shrinking—to 0.98.

图41.单个表面的透射也可以分为三个步骤，每个步骤都伴随着收缩和/或转弯。长度为 0.98 的箭头的面积约为 0.96，表示光线的透射概率为 96%（加上 4% 的反射概率，总计为 100%）。

FIGURE 41. Transmission by a single surface can also be divided into three steps, with a shrink and/or turn for each step. An arrow of length 0.98 has a square of about 0.96, representing a probabilty of transmission of 96% (which, combined with the 4% probability of reflection, accounts for 100%) of the light).

第三步——光子穿过玻璃内部——涉及额外的旋转，但没有收缩。

The third step—the photon going through the interior of the glass—involves additional turning and no shrinking.

最终结果是一个长度为 0.98 的箭头，它朝某个方向旋转，其平方表示光子到达 B 的概率——96%。

The net result is an arrow of length 0.98 turned in some direction, whose square represents the probability that a photon will arrive at B—96%.

现在我们再来看两个表面的部分反射。前表面的反射与单个表面的反射相同，因此前表面反射的三个步骤与我们刚才看到的相同（图 40）。

Now let’s look at partial reflection by two surfaces again. Reflection from the front surface is the same as for a single surface, so the three steps for front surface reflection are the same as we saw a moment ago (Fig. 40).

背面反射可以分解为七个步骤（见图42）。它包括转动，转动量等于秒表指针在整个距离上计时的光子转数（步骤 1、3、5 和 7），缩小到 0.2（步骤 4），以及两次缩小到 0.98（步骤 2 和 6）。最终形成的箭头方向与之前相同，但长度约为 0.192（0.98 * 0.2 * 0.98），我在第一讲中将其近似为 0.2。

Reflection from the back surface can be broken down into seven steps (see Fig. 42). It involves turning equal to the total amount of turning of the stopwatch hand timing a photon over the entire distance (steps 1, 3, 5, and 7), shrinking to 0.2 (step 4), and two shrinks to 0.98 (steps 2 and 6). The resulting arrow ends up in the same direction as before, but the length is about 0.192 (0.98 * 0.2 * 0.98), which I approximated as 0.2 in the first lecture.

图42.从玻璃层背面反射光线的过程可以分为七个步骤。步骤 1、3、5 和 7 仅涉及旋转；步骤 2 和 6 涉及收缩至 0.98，步骤 4 涉及收缩至 0.2。最终得到一个长度为 0.192 的箭头（在第一讲中近似为 0.2），其旋转角度与假想的秒表指针旋转的总角度相对应。

FIGURE 42. Reflection from the back surface of a layer of glass can be divided into seven steps. Steps 1,3,5, and 7 involve turning only; steps 2 and 6 involve shrinks to 0.98, and step 4 involves a shrink to 0.2. The result is an arrow of length 0.192—which was approximated as 0.2 in the first lecture—turned at an angle that corresponds to the total amount of turning by the imaginary stopwatch hand.

综上所述，以下是光在玻璃上反射和透射的规则：1）从空气反射回空气（从前表面反射）需要收缩到 0.2 并旋转半圈；2）从玻璃反射回玻璃（从后表面反射）也需要收缩到 0.2，但不需要旋转；3）从空气透射到玻璃或从玻璃透射到空气都需要收缩到 0.98，并且在两种情况下都不需要旋转。

In summary, here are the rules for reflection and transmission of light by glass: 1) reflection from air back to air (off a front surface) involves a shrink to 0.2 and half a turn; 2) reflection from glass back to glass (off a back surface) also involves a shrink to 0.2, but no turning; and 3) transmission from air to glass or from glass to air involves a shrink to 0.98 and no turning in either case.

或许有点过犹不及，但我还是忍不住要再举一个有趣的例子，来说明这些循序渐进的规则是如何运作和分析的。让我们把探测器移到玻璃下方，并考虑一下我们在第一讲中没有讨论的内容——两层玻璃表面的透射概率（见图 43）。

Perhaps it is too much of a good thing, but I cannot resist showing you a cute further example of how things work and are analyzed by these rules of successive steps. Let us move the detector to a location below the glass, and consider something we didn’t talk about in the first lecture—the probability of transmission by two surfaces of glass (see Fig. 43).

你当然知道答案：概率光子到达 B 点的概率等于 100% 减去到达 A 点的概率，后者我们已经事先计算过了。因此，如果我们发现到达 A 点的概率是 7%，那么到达 B 点的概率就一定是 93%。由于到达 A 点的概率会随着玻璃厚度的变化而变化，从 0% 到 8% 再到 16%，所以到达 B 点的概率也会从 100% 到 92% 再到 84%。

Of course you know the answer: the probability of a photon to arrive at B is simply 100% minus the probability to arrive at A, which we worked out beforehand. Thus, if we found the chance to arrive at A is 7%, the chance to arrive at B must be 93%. And as the chance for A varies from zero through 8% to 16% (due to the different thicknesses of glass), the chance for B changes from 100% through 92% to 84%.

图43.两表面透射过程可分为五个步骤。步骤 2 将单位箭头缩短至 0.98，步骤 4 将 0.98 的箭头缩短至其 0.98 的 0.98倍（约 0.96）；步骤 1、3 和 5 仅涉及旋转。由此得到的长度为 0.96 的箭头，其边长约为 0.92，代表两表面透射概率为 92%（这对应于预期的 8% 反射率，但仅“每天两次”才正确）。当层厚合适，使得反射概率为 16%，透射概率为 92% 时，竟然有 108% 的光被解释！这个分析肯定有问题！

FIGURE 43. Transmission by two surfaces can be broken down into five steps. Step 2 shrinks the unit arrow to 0.98, step 4 shrinks the 0.98 arrow to 0.98 of that (about 0.96); steps 1,3, and 5 involve turning only. The resulting arrow of length 0.96 has a square of about 0.92, representing a probability of transmission by two surfaces of 92% (which corresponds to the expected 8% reflection, which is right only “twice a day”). When the thickness of the layer is right to produce a probability of 16% reflection, with a 92%) probability of transmission, 108%) of the light is accounted for! Something is wrong with this analysis!

答案没错，但我们期望通过对最终箭头进行平方来计算所有概率。那么，如何计算玻璃层透射的振幅箭头呢？它的长度又是如何恰好与每种情况下 A 的长度相匹配，从而使 A 的概率和 B 的概率之和始终恰好为 100% 的呢？让我们来详细探讨一下。

That is the right answer, but we are expecting to calculate all probabilities by squaring a final arrow. How do we calculate the amplitude arrow for transmission by a layer of glass, and how does it manage to vary in length so appropriately as to fit with the length for A in each case, so the probability for A and the probability for B always add up to exactly 100%? Let us look a little into the details.

光子从光源到达玻璃下方的探测器 B 点，需要经过五个步骤。我们边走边缩小并旋转单位箭头。

For a photon to go from the source to the detector below the glass, at B, five steps are involved. Let’s shrink and turn the unit arrow as we go along.

前三个步骤与前一个例子相同：光子从光源传播到玻璃（旋转，不收缩）；光子被透射……前表面（不旋转，收缩至 0.98）；光子穿过玻璃（旋转，不收缩）。

The first three steps are the same as in the previous example: the photon goes from the source to the glass (turning, no shrinking); the photon is transmitted by the front surface (no turning, shrinking to 0.98); the photon goes through the glass (turning, no shrinking).

第四步——光子穿过玻璃的背面——与第二步相同，就收缩和转弯而言：没有转弯，但收缩到 0.98 的 0.98，因此箭头现在的长度为 0.96。

The fourth step—the photon passes through the back surface of the glass—is the same as the second step, as far as shrinks and turns go: no turns, but a shrinkage to 0.98 of the 0.98, so the arrow now has a length of 0.96.

最后，光子再次穿过空气，到达探测器——这意味着它还要再转几圈，但不会再缩小。最终得到一个长度为0.96的箭头，其指向由秒表指针的转动次数决定的方向。

Finally, the photon goes through the air again, down to the detector—that means more turning, but no further shrinking. The result is an arrow of length 0.96, pointing in some direction determined by the successive turnings of the stopwatch hand.

长度为 0.96 的箭头代表约 92% 的概率（0.96 的平方），这意味着每 100 个从光源发出的光子中，平均有 92 个到达 B 点。这也意味着 8% 的光子被两个表面反射并到达 A 点。但我们在第一讲中发现，两个表面 8% 的反射率并非总是成立（“一天两次”）——实际上，随着玻璃层厚度的稳定增加，两个表面的反射率会在 0% 到 16% 之间循环波动。当玻璃的厚度恰好足以产生 16% 的部分反射时会发生什么？每 100 个从光源发出的光子中，有 16 个到达 A 点，92 个到达 B 点，这意味着 108% 的光都被反射了——太可怕了！肯定有什么地方不对劲。

An arrow whose length is 0.96 represents a probability of about 92% (0.96 squared), which means an average of 92 photons reach B out of every 100 that leave the source. That also means that 8% of the photons are reflected by the two surfaces and reach A. But we found out in the first lecture that an 8% reflection by two surfaces is only right sometimes (“twice a day”)—that in reality, the reflection by two surfaces fluctuates in a cycle from zero to 16% as the thickness of the layer steadily increases. What happens when the glass is just the right thickness to make a partial reflection of 16%? For every 100 photons that leave the source, 16 arrive at A and 92 arrive at B, which means 108% of the light has been accounted for—horrifying! Something is wrong.

我们忽略了光线到达 B 点的所有路径！例如，光线可以从后表面反射，向上穿过玻璃，就像要到达 A 点一样，但随后又从前表面反射，向下照射到 B 点（见图44）。这条路径需要九个步骤。让我们看看当光线经过每个步骤时，单位箭头会发生什么变化（别担心，它只会变短和转弯！）。

We neglected to consider all the ways the light could get to B! For instance, it could bounce off the back surface, go up through the glass as if it were going to A, but then reflect off the front surface, back down toward B (see Fig. 44). This path takes nine steps. Let’s see what happens successively to the unit arrow as the light goes through each step (don’t worry; it only shrinks and turns!).

第一步——光子穿过空气——发生转向；体积不缩小。第二步——光子穿过玻璃——不发生转向，但体积缩小至0.98。第三步——光子……穿过玻璃——旋转；不收缩。第四步——从背面反射——不旋转，但收缩至 0.98 的 0.2，即 0.196。第五步——光子再次穿过玻璃向上运动——旋转；不收缩。第六步——光子从正面（实际上是“背面”，因为光子仍然留在玻璃内部）反射——不旋转，但收缩至 0.196 的 0.2，即 0.0392。第七步——光子再次穿过玻璃向下运动——继续旋转；不收缩。第八步——光子穿过背面——不旋转，但收缩至 0.0392 的 0.98，即 0.0384。最后，第九步——光子穿过空气到达探测器——旋转；不收缩。

First step—photon goes through the air—turning; no shrinking. Second step—photon passes through the glass—no turning, but shrinking to 0.98. Third step—photon goes through the glass—turning; no shrinking. Fourth step—reflection off the back surface—no turning, but shrinking to 0.2 of 0.98, or 0.196. Fifth step—photon goes back up through the glass—turning; no shrinking. Sixth step—photon bounces off front surface (it’s really a “back” surface, because the photon stays inside the glass)—no turning, but shrinking to 0.2 of 0.196, or 0.0392. Seventh step—photon goes back down through glass—more turning; no shrinking. Eighth step—photon passes through back surface—no turning, but shrinking to 0.98 of 0.0392, or 0.0384. Finally, the ninth step—photon goes through air to detector—turning; no shrinking.

图44.为了使计算更加精确，必须考虑光线在两个表面之间传输的另一种方式。该路径涉及两次 0.98 的收缩（步骤 2 和 8）和两次 0.2 的收缩（步骤 4 和 6），最终形成长度为 0.0384（四舍五入为 0.04）的箭头。

FIGURE 44. Another way that light could be transmitted by two surfaces must be considered in order to make the calculation more accurate. This path involves two shrinks of 0.98 (steps 2 and 8) and two shrinks of 0.2 (steps 4 and 6), resulting in an arrow of length 0.0384 (rounded off to 0.04).

所有这些收缩和旋转的结果是一个长度为 0.0384 的振幅——为了方便起见，我们姑且称之为 0.04——其旋转角度对应于秒表在计时光子通过这条更长路径时旋转的总角度。这个箭头代表光从光源到达 B 的第二条路径。现在我们有两种选择，因此必须将两条箭头相加——一条代表更直接路径的箭头，其长度为 0.96；另一条代表更长路径的箭头，其长度为 0.04——才能得到最终的箭头。

The result of all this shrinking and turning is an amplitude of length 0.0384—call it 0.04, for all practical purposes—and turned at an angle that corresponds to the total amount of turning by the stopwatch as it times the photon going through this longer path. This arrow represents a second way that light can get from the source to B. Now we have two alternatives, so we must add the two arrows—the arrow for the more direct path, whose length is 0.96, and the arrow for the longer way, whose length is 0.04—to make the final arrow.

这两个箭头通常方向不同，因为改变玻璃的厚度会改变0.04箭头和0.96箭头的相对方向。但请注意，结果非常巧妙：秒表在步骤3和5（光子到达A点）计时时多转的圈数，恰好等于它在步骤5和7（光子到达B点）计时时多转的圈数。这意味着，当两个反射箭头相互抵消，最终形成一个代表零反射的箭头时，透射箭头相互增强，形成一个长度为0.96 + 0.04，即1的箭头——当反射概率为零时，透射概率为100%（见图45）。当反射箭头相互加强，振幅为 0.4 时，透射箭头则相互抵消，振幅为 0.96 - 0.04，即 0.92。此时，反射率计算为 16%，透射率计算为 84%（0.92 的平方）。由此可见，大自然的法则多么巧妙，确保我们总能计算出 100% 的光子！

The two arrows are usually not in the same direction, because changing the thickness of the glass changes the relative direction of the 0.04 arrow to the 0.96 arrow. But look how nicely things work out: the extra turns made by the stopwatch timing a photon during steps 3 and 5 (on its way to A) are exactly equal to the extra turns it makes timing a photon during steps 5 and 7 (on its way to B). That means when the two reflection arrows are cancelling each other to make a final arrow representing zero reflection, the arrows for transmission are reinforcing each other to make an arrow of length 0.96 + 0.04, or 1—when the probability of reflection is zero, the probability of transmission is 100% (see Fig. 45). And when the arrows for reflection are reinforcing each other to make an amplitude of 0.4, the arrows for transmission are going against each other, making an amplitude of length 0.96 — 0.04, or 0.92—when reflection is calculated to be 16%, transmission is calculated to be 84% (0.92 squared). You see how clever Nature is with Her rules to make sure that we always come out with 100% of the photons accounted for!5

图45.大自然总是确保 100% 的光线都被利用。当厚度合适，透射箭头能够聚集时，反射箭头就会相互抵消；当反射箭头聚集时，透射箭头就会相互抵消。

FIGURE 45. Nature always makes sure 100% of the light is accounted for. When the thickness is right for the transmission arrows to accumulate, the arrows for reflection oppose each other; when the arrows for reflection accumulate, the arrows for transmission oppose each other.

最后，在我结束之前，我想告诉大家，关于何时需要乘以箭头的规则还有一个扩展：箭头不仅要乘以由一系列步骤组成的事件，还要乘以由多个同时发生的事件组成的事件——这些事件彼此独立，也可能同时发生。例如，假设我们有两个光源 X 和 Y，以及两个探测器 A 和 B（见图47），我们想要计算以下事件的概率：X 和 Y 各自失去一个光子后，A 和 B 各自获得一个光子。

Finally, before I go, I would like to tell you that there is an extension to the rule that tells us when to multiply arrows: arrows are to be multiplied not only for an event that consists of a succession of steps, but also for an event that consists of a number of things happening concomitantly—independently and possibly simultaneously. For example, suppose we have two sources, X and Y, and two detectors, A and B (see Fig. 47), and we want to calculate the probability for the following event: after X and Y each lose a photon, A and B each gain a photon.

图46.为了更精确地计算，还应考虑光反射的其他方式。图中，步骤 2 和 10 处收缩了 0.98；步骤 4、6 和 8 处收缩了 0.2。结果是一个长度约为 0.008 的箭头，这是反射的另一种可能方式，因此应将其添加到代表反射的其他箭头中（前表面为 0.2，后表面为 0.192）。

FIGURE 46. Yet other ways the light could reflect should be considered for a more accurate calculation. In this figure, shrinks of 0.98 occur at steps 2 and 10; shrinks of 0.2 occur at steps 4, 6, and 8. The result is an arrow with a length of about 0.008, which is another alternative for reflection, and should therefore be added to the other arrows which represent reflection (0.2 for the front surface and 0.192 for the back surface).

在这个例子中，光子穿过空间到达探测器——它们既不被反射也不被透射——所以现在是时候不再忽略光在传播过程中会扩散这一事实了。现在我向你们展示单色光在空间中从一点传播到另一点的完整规则——这里没有任何近似值，也没有任何简化。这就是单色光在空间中传播（忽略偏振）需要知道的一切：箭头的角度取决于假想的秒表指针，它每英寸旋转一定的次数（取决于光子的颜色）；箭头的长度与光传播的距离成反比——换句话说，箭头随着光传播而缩短。6

In this example, the photons travel through space to get to the detectors—they are neither reflected nor transmitted—so now is a good time for me to stop disregarding the fact that light spreads out as it goes along. I now present you with the complete rule for monochromatic light travelling from one point to another through space—there is nothing approximate here, and no simplification. This is all there is to know about monochromatic light going through space (disregarding polarization): the angle of the arrow depends on the imaginary stopwatch hand, which rotates a certain number of times per inch (depending on the color of the photon); the length of the arrow is inversely proportional to the distance the light goes—in other words, the arrow shrinks as the light goes along.6

图47.如果某个特定事件发生的途径之一取决于若干独立事件的发生，则该途径的振幅可通过将这些独立事件的箭头相乘来计算。在本例中，最终事件为：光源 X 和 Y 各自失去一个光子后，光电倍增管 A 和 B 发出咔嗒声。该事件发生的一种途径是，一个光子从 X 到 A，另一个光子从 Y 到 B（两个独立事件）。为了计算这种“第一种途径”的概率，需要将每个独立事件（X 到 A 和 Y 到 B）的箭头相乘，从而得出该特定途径的振幅。（分析继续见图 48。）

FIGURE 47. If one of the ways a particular event can happen depends on a number of things happening independently, the amplitude for this way is calculated by multiplying the arrows of the independent things. In this case, the final event is: after sources X and Y each lose a photon, photomultipliers A and B make a click. One way this event could happen is that a photon could go from X to A and a photon could go from Y to B (two independent things). To calculate the probability for this “first way, the arrows for each independent thing—X to A and Y to B—are multiplied to produce the amplitude for this particular way. (Analysis continued in Fig. 48.)

假设从 X 到 A 的箭头长度为 0.5，指向 5 点钟方向，从 Y 到 B 的箭头也是如此（图 47）。将一个箭头乘以另一个箭头，我们得到最终长度为 0.25 的箭头，指向 10 点钟方向。

Let’s suppose the arrow for X to A is 0.5 in length and is pointing toward 5 o’clock, as is the arrow for Y to B (Fig. 47). Multiplying one arrow by the other, we get a final arrow of length 0.25, pointed at 10 o’clock.

图48。图47中描述的事件发生的另一种方式——一个光子从 X 到 B，另一个光子从 Y 到 A——也取决于两个独立事件的发生，因此这种“第二种方式”的振幅也是通过将这两个独立事件的箭头相乘来计算的。“第一种方式”和“第二种方式”的箭头最终相加，得到事件的最终箭头。无论绘制、相乘和相加多少个箭头，事件的概率始终由一个最终箭头表示。

FIGURE 48. The other way the event described in Figure 47 could happen—a photon goes from X to B and a photon goes from Y to A—also depends on two independent things happening, so the amplitude for this “second way” is also calculated by multiplying the arrows of the independent things. The “first way” and “second way” arrows are ultimately added together, resulting in the final arrow for the event. The probability of an event is always represented by a single final arrow—no matter how many arrows were drawn, multiplied, and added to achieve it.

等等！还有另一种发生方式：来自 X 的光子可以到达 B，来自 Y 的光子可以到达 A。这些子事件各有其振幅，因此必须绘制并相乘这些箭头，才能得出这种特定发生方式的振幅（见图48）。由于距离上的收缩量与旋转量相比非常小，因此从 X 到 B 和从 Y 到 A 的箭头长度与其他箭头基本相同，均为 0.5，但它们的旋转角度却截然不同：对于红光，秒表指针每英寸旋转 36,000 次，因此即使距离上的微小差异也会导致时间上的显著差异。

But wait! There is another way this event could happen: the photon from X could go to B, and the photon from Y could go to A. Each of these subevents has an amplitude, and these arrows must also be drawn and multiplied to produce an amplitude for this particular way the event could happen (see Fig. 48). Since the amount of shrinkage over distance is very small compared to the amount of turning, the arrows from X to B and Y to A have essentially the same length as the other arrows, 0.5, but their turning is quite different: the stopwatch hand rotates 36,000 times per inch for red light, so even a tiny difference in distance results in a substantial difference in timing.

将事件可能发生的每种方式的振幅相加，即可得到最终的箭头。由于它们的长度基本相同，如果方向相反，则箭头可能会相互抵消。可以通过改变源或探测器之间的距离来改变两个箭头的相对方向：只需稍微拉开或靠近探测器，就可以使事件发生的概率增大或完全抵消，就像两个表面部分反射的情况一样：7

The amplitudes for each way the event could happen are added to produce the final arrow. Since their lengths are essentially the same, it is possible for the arrows to cancel each other out if their directions are opposed to each other. The relative directions of the two arrows can be changed by changing the distance between the sources or the detectors: simply moving the detectors apart or together a little bit can make the probability of the event amplify or completely cancel out, just as in the case of partial reflection by two surfaces:7

在这个例子中，箭头先相乘，然后相加，最终得到一个箭头（事件的振幅），其平方即为事件的概率。需要强调的是，无论我们绘制、相加或相乘多少个箭头，我们的目标都是计算出事件的最终箭头。物理系学生一开始常常犯错，就是因为他们没有牢记这一点。他们长时间分析涉及单个光子的事件，以至于开始认为箭头是……某种程度上与光子相关。但这些箭头代表概率振幅，其平方值即为完整事件发生的概率。8

In this example, arrows were multiplied and then added to produce a final arrow (the amplitude for the event), whose square is the probability of the event. It is to be emphasized that no matter how many arrows we draw, add, or multiply, our objective is to calculate a single final arrow for the event. Mistakes are often made by physics students at first because they do not keep this important point in mind. They work for so long analyzing events involving a single photon that they begin to think that the arrow is somehow associated with the photon. But these arrows are probability amplitudes, that give, when squared, the probability of a complete event.8

在下一节课中，我将开始简化和解释物质的性质——解释为什么体积会缩小到0.2，为什么光在玻璃或水中传播的速度似乎比在空气中慢等等——因为我之前一直有所隐瞒：光子实际上并不是从玻璃表面反射的；它们与玻璃内部的电子相互作用。我会向你们展示光子是如何从一个电子传递到另一个电子的，以及反射和透射实际上是电子吸收一个光子后，就像“挠挠头”一样，释放出一个新的光子的结果。这种对我们之前讨论的所有内容的简化非常巧妙。

In the next lecture I will begin the process of simplifying and explaining the properties of matter—to explain where the shrinking to 0.2 comes from, why light appears to go slower through glass or water than through air, and so on—because I have been cheating so far: the photons don’t really bounce off the surface of the glass; they interact with the electrons inside the glass. I’ll show you how photons do nothing but go from one electron to another, and how reflection and transmission are really the result of an electron picking up a photon, “scratching its head,” so to speak, and emitting a new photon. This simplification of everything we have talked about so far is very pretty.

1.当箭头指向相反方向的区域被擦除时，镜面上箭头大致指向左侧的区域也会产生强烈的反射。只有当偏左和偏右的区域同时反射时，它们才会相互抵消。这类似于两个表面的部分反射：虽然每个表面都会单独反射，但如果两个表面的厚度使得它们产生的箭头指向相反的方向，则反射就会相互抵消。

1 The areas of the mirror whose arrows point generally to the left also make a strong reflection (when the areas whose arrows point the other way are erased). It’s when both left-biased and right-biased areas reflect together that they cancel out. This is analogous to the case of partial reflection by two surfaces: while either surface will reflect on its own, if the thickness is such that the two surfaces contribute arrows pointing in opposite directions, reflection is cancelled out.

2我忍不住要跟你们说说大自然创造的一种光栅：盐晶体是由钠原子和氯原子以规则的排列方式构成的。它们交替排列的图案，就像我们手上的沟槽一样，当特定颜色的光（在本例中是X射线）照射到上面时，就会像光栅一样产生衍射。通过找到探测器接收到大量这种特殊反射（称为衍射）的特定位置，人们可以精确地确定沟槽之间的距离，从而确定原子之间的距离（见图28）。这是一种测定各种晶体结构以及证实X射线与光本质相同的绝妙方法。这类实验最早于1914年进行。能够第一次如此详细地观察到不同物质中原子的排列方式，真是令人兴奋不已。

2 I can’t resist telling you about a grating that Nature has made: salt crystals are sodium and chlorine atoms packed in a regular pattern. Their alternating pattern, like our grooved surface, acts like a grating when light of the right color (X-rays, in this case) shines on it. By finding the specific locations where a detector picks up a lot of this special reflection (called diffraction), one can determine exactly how far apart the grooves are, and thus how far apart the atoms are (see Fig. 28). It is a beautiful way of determining the structure of all kinds of crystals as well as confirming that X-rays are the same thing as light. Such experiments were first done in 1914. It was very exciting to see, in detail, for the first time how the atoms are packed together in different substances.

3这就是“不确定性原理”的一个例子：光线在方块间传播的路径与它之后的传播路径之间存在一种“互补性”——精确掌握这两者是不可能的。我想把不确定性原理放在它的历史背景下：当量子物理学的革命性思想最初出现时，人们仍然试图用传统的观念（例如，光沿直线传播）来理解它们。但到了某个阶段，这些传统的观念就会失效，因此出现了一种警告，其实质是：“当……的时候，你的传统观念就完全行不通了。” 如果你摒弃所有传统的观念，转而使用我在这些讲座中解释的观念——用箭头表示事件发生的所有可能方式——那么就不需要不确定性原理了！

3 This is an example of the “uncertainty principle”: there is a kind of “complementarity” between knowledge of where the light goes between the blocks and where it goes afterwards—precise knowledge of both is impossible. I would like to put the uncertainty principle in its historical place: When the revolutionary ideas of quantum physics were first coming out, people still tried to understand them in terms of old-fashioned ideas (such as, light goes in straight lines). But at a certain point the old-fashioned ideas would begin to fail, so a warning was developed that said, in effect, “Your old-fashioned ideas are no damn good when …” If you get rid of all the old-fashioned ideas and instead use the ideas that I’m explaining in these lectures—adding arrows for all the ways an event can happen—there is no need for an uncertainty principle!

4.数学家们试图找到所有符合代数规则（A + B = B + A，A * B = B * A，等等）的物体。这些规则最初是为正整数制定的，用于计数苹果或人之类的东西。随着零、分数、无理数（不能表示为两个整数之比的数）和负数的出现，数字得到了改进，并且仍然遵循最初的代数规则。数学家们发明的一些数字起初给人们带来了困难——半个人的概念很难想象——但如今，这完全不成问题：当人们听说某些地区平均每平方英里有3.2人时，没有人会感到任何道德上的不安或令人不适的血腥感。他们不会试图去想象那0.2个人；相反，他们知道3.2的含义：如果将3.2乘以10，就得到32。因此，一些符合代数规则的事物即使不总是代表真实情况，也能引起数学家的兴趣。平面上的箭头可以通过将一个箭头的箭头指向另一个箭头的尾部来“相加”，或者通过连续的旋转和收缩来“相乘”。由于这些箭头遵循与普通数字相同的代数规则，数学家称它们为数字。但为了将它们与普通数字区分开来，它们被称为“复数”。对于那些学习过足够多的数学知识，接触过复数的人来说，我本可以这样说：“一个事件的概率是复数的绝对值的平方。当一个事件可以以多种方式发生时，将复数相加；当它只能以一系列步骤发生时，将复数相乘。”虽然这样说听起来更令人印象深刻，但我并没有说更多——我只是换了一种说法。

4 Mathematicians have tried to find all the objects one could possibly find that obey the rules of algebra (A + B = B + A, A * B = B*A, and so on). The rules were originally made for positive integers, used for counting things like apples or people. Numbers were improved with the invention of zero, fractions, irrational numbers—numbers that cannot be expressed as a ratio of two integers—and negative numbers, and continued to obey the original rules of algebra. Some of the numbers that mathematicians invented posed difficulties for people at first—the idea of half a person was difficult to imagine—but today, there’s no difficulty at all: nobody has any moral qualms or discomforting gory feelings when they hear that there is an average of 3.2 people per square mile in some regions. They don’t try to imagine the 0.2 people; rather, they know what 3.2 means: if they multiply 3.2 by 10, they get 32. Thus, some things that satisfy the rules of algebra can be interesting to mathematicians even though they don’t always represent a real situation. Arrows on a plane can be “added” by putting the head of one arrow on the tail of another, or “multiplied” by successive turns and shrinks. Since these arrows obey the same rules of algebra as regular numbers, mathematicians call them numbers. But to distinguish them from ordinary numbers, they’re called “complex numbers.” For those of you who have studied mathematics enough to have come to complex numbers, I could have said, “the probability of an event is the absolute square of a complex number. When an event can happen in alternative ways, you add the complex numbers; when it can happen only as a succession of steps, you multiply the complex numbers.” Although it may sound more impressive that way, I have not said any more than I did before—I just used a different language.

5你会注意到，我们将 0.0384 改为 0.04，并将 84% 用作 0.92 的平方，以确保 100% 的光都被考虑在内。但当所有数据加在一起时，0.0384 和 84% 无需四舍五入——所有细小的箭头（代表光线传播的所有路径）相互抵消，从而保证答案正确。对于喜欢这类计算的朋友，这里举一个例子，说明光线从光源到 A 点探测器的另一种传播路径——经过三次反射（和两次透射），最终形成长度为 0.98 * 0.2 * 0.2 * 0.2 * 0.98 的箭头，约为 0.008——一个非常小的箭头（见图46）。要完整计算两个表面的部分反射，你需要加上那个小箭头，再加上一个更小的箭头来表示五次反射，依此类推。

5 You’ll notice that we changed 0.0384 to 0.04 and used 84% as the square of 0.92, in order to make 100% of the light accounted for. But when everything is added together, 0.0384 and 84% don’t have to be rounded off—all the little bits and pieces of arrows (representing all the ways the light could go) compensate for each other and keep the answer correct. For those of you who like this sort of thing, here is an example of another way that the light could go from the light source to the detector at A—a series of three reflections (and two transmissions), resulting in a final arrow of length 0.98 * 0.2 * 0.2 * 0.2 * 0.98, or about 0.008—a very tiny arrow (see Fig. 46). To make a complete calculation of partial reflection by two surfaces, you would have to add in that small arrow, plus an even smaller one that represents five reflections, and so on.

6这条规则与学校里教的内容相符——光线在一定距离内传输的量与距离的平方成反比——因为缩小到原来一半大小的箭头，其正方形的面积就变成了原来的四分之一。

6 This rule checks out with what they teach in school—the amount of light transmitted over a distance varies inversely as the square of the distance—because an arrow that shrinks to half its original size has a square one-fourth as big.

7这种现象被称为汉伯里-布朗-特维斯效应，它被用来区分深空中的单个无线电波源和双源，即使这两个源非常接近。

7 This phenomenon, called the Hanbury-Brown-Twiss effect, has been used to distinguish between a single source and a double source of radio waves in deep space, even when the two sources are extremely close together.

8牢记这一原理应该有助于学生避免被“波包的简化”和类似的魔法所迷惑。

8 Keeping this principle in mind should help the student avoid being confused by things such as the “reduction of a wave packet” and similar magic.

3

3

电子及其相互作用

Electrons and Their Interactions

这是关于量子电动力学理论这一相当艰深主题的四讲中的第三讲。今晚到场的人数显然比之前多，所以你们当中有些人可能没听过前两讲，会觉得这讲几乎难以理解。听过前两讲的你们也会觉得这讲难以理解，但你们知道这没关系：正如我在第一讲中所解释的，我们描述自然的方式通常对我们来说是难以理解的。

This is the third of four lectures on a rather difficult subject—the theory of quantum electrodynamics—and since there are obviously more people here tonight than there were before, some of you haven’t heard the other two lectures and will find this lecture almost incomprehensible. Those of you who have heard the other two lectures will also find this lecture incomprehensible, but you know that that’s all right: as I explained in the first lecture, the way we have to describe Nature is generally incomprehensible to us.

在这些讲座中，我想和大家聊聊我们最熟悉的物理学部分——光与电子的相互作用。你们所熟悉的绝大多数现象都与光和电子的相互作用有关——例如，所有的化学和生物学。唯一不在这个理论涵盖范围内的现象是引力现象和核现象；其他一切都包含在这个理论之中。

In these lectures I want to tell you about the part of physics that we know best, the interaction of light and electrons. Most of the phenomena you are familiar with involve the interaction of light and electrons—all of chemistry and biology, for example. The only phenomena that are not covered by this theory are phenomena of gravitation and nuclear phenomena; everything else is contained in this theory.

我们在第一讲中发现，我们甚至没有令人满意的机制来描述最简单的现象，例如玻璃对光的部分反射。我们也无法预测给定的光子会被玻璃反射还是透射。我们所能做的只是计算特定事件发生的概率——在这种情况下，光线是否会被反射取决于具体情况。（当光线垂直照射到玻璃的单个表面上时，反射率约为 4%；光线以更大的倾斜角度照射到玻璃上时，反射概率会增加。）

We found out in the first lecture that we have no satisfactory mechanism to describe even the simplest of phenomena, such as partial reflection of light by glass. We also have no way to predict whether a given photon will be reflected or transmitted by the glass. All we can do is calculate the probability that a particular event will happen—whether the light will be reflected, in this case. (This is about 4%, when the light shines straight down on a single surface of glass; the probability of reflection increases as the light hits the glass at more of a slant.)

在通常情况下处理概率时，有以下“组合规则”：1）如果某事可以以多种方式发生，我们将每种不同方式的概率相加；2）如果事件是一系列步骤发生的——或者取决于许多“同时”（独立地）发生的事情——那么我们将每个步骤（或事情）的概率相乘。

When we deal with probabilities under ordinary circumstances, there are the following “rules of composition”: 1) if something can happen in alternative ways, we add the probabilities for each of the different ways; 2) if the event occurs as a succession of steps—or depends on a number of things happening “concomitantly” (independently)—then we multiply the probabilities of each of the steps (or things).

在奇妙的量子物理世界里，概率的计算方法是将概率乘以箭头长度的平方：在通常情况下，我们本应将概率相加，而在这里，我们却发现需要“相加”箭头；在通常情况下，我们本应将概率相乘，而在这里，我们却需要“相乘”箭头。这种计算概率的方法得到的奇特结果与实验结果完美契合。我很高兴我们必须借助如此奇特的规则和推理才能理解自然，我也乐于与人分享这些。这种对自然的分析背后并没有什么“齿轮和机关”；如果你想理解自然，就必须接受这种方法。

In the wild and wonderful world of quantum physics, probabilities are calculated as the square of the length of an arrow: where we would have expected to add the probabilities under ordinary circumstances, we find ourselves “adding” arrows; where we normally would have multiplied the probabilities, we “multiply” arrows. The peculiar answers that we get from calculating probabilities in this manner match perfectly the results of experiment. I’m rather delighted that we must resort to such peculiar rules and strange reasoning in order to understand Nature, and I enjoy telling people about it. There are no “wheels and gears” beneath this analysis of Nature; if you want to understand Her, this is what you have to take.

在正式开始本次讲座之前，我想先给大家展示另一个光行为的例子。我想讨论的是一束非常微弱的单色光——一次只有一个光子——从光源 S 点射向探测器 D 点（见图49）。我们在光源和探测器之间放置一块屏风，并在 A 点和 B 点各开一个相距几毫米的小孔。（如果光源和探测器之间的距离是 100 厘米，那么孔径必须小于十分之一毫米。）我们把 A 点放在与 S 点和 D 点成一条直线上，把 B 点放在 A 点旁边，而不是与 S 点和 D 点成一条直线上。

Before I go into the main part of this lecture, I’d like to show you another example of how light behaves. What I would like to talk about is very weak light of one color—one photon at a time—going from a source, at S, to a detector, at D (see Fig. 49). Let’s put a screen in between the source and the detector and make two very tiny holes a few millimeters apart from each other, at A and B. (If the source and detector are 100 centimeters apart, the holes have to be smaller than a tenth of a millimeter.) Let’s put A in line with S and D, and put B somewhere to the side of A, not in line with S and D.

当我们关闭 B 点的孔时，探测器 D 会接收到一定数量的咔嗒声——这代表了穿过 A 点的光子数（假设探测器平均每接收到 100 个离开 S 点的光子就会产生一次咔嗒声，即 1%）。当我们关闭 A 点的孔并打开 B 点的孔时，根据第二讲的内容，我们平均会得到几乎相同的咔嗒声，因为孔非常小。（当我们过度“压缩”光时，通常世界的规则——例如光沿直线传播——就会失效。）当我们同时打开两个孔时，会得到一个复杂的结果，因为存在干涉现象：如果两个孔之间的距离是固定的，我们会得到比预期 2% 更多的咔嗒声（最大值约为 4%）；如果两个孔之间的距离略有不同，则完全不会产生咔嗒声。

When we close the hole at B, we get a certain number of clicks at D—which represents the photons that came through A (let’s say the detector clicks an average of one time for every 100 photons that leave S, or 1%). When we close the hole at A and open the hole at B, we know from the second lecture that we get nearly the same number of clicks, on average, because the holes are so small. (When we “squeeze” light too much, the rules of the ordinary world—such as light goes in straight lines—fall apart.) When we open both holes we get a complicated answer, because interference is present: If the holes are a certain distance apart, we get more clicks than the expected 2% (the maximum is about 4%); if the two holes are a slightly different distance apart, we get no clicks at all.

图49.光源 S 和探测器 D 之间的屏幕上有两个小孔（分别位于 A 和 B），当其中一个孔打开时，通过的光量几乎相同（在本例中为 1%）。当两个孔都打开时，就会发生“干涉”：探测器发出咔嗒声的概率从 0% 到 4% 不等，具体取决于 A 和 B 之间的距离——如图 51 (a)所示。

FIGURE 49. Two tiny holes (at A and B) in a screen that is between a source S and a detector D let nearly the same amount of light through (in this case 1%) when one or the other hole is open. When both holes are open, “interference”, occurs: the detector clicks from zero to 4% of the time, depending on the separation of A and B—shown in Figure 51 (a).

人们通常会认为，打开第二个孔总是会增加到达探测器的光量，但实际情况并非如此。因此，说光线“要么朝这个方向，要么朝那个方向”是错误的。我仍然会不自觉地说，“嗯，它要么朝这个方向，要么朝那个方向”，但当我这么说的时候，我必须记住……我的意思是，从振幅叠加的角度来看：光子既有朝一个方向传播的振幅，也有朝另一个方向传播的振幅。如果这两个振幅相互抵消，光就无法到达目的地——即使在这种情况下，两个孔都处于打开状态。

One would normally think that opening a second hole would always increase the amount of light reaching the detector, but that’s not what actually happens. And so saying that the light goes “either one way or the other” is false. I still catch myself saying, “Well, it goes either this way or that way,” but when I say that, I have to keep in mind that I mean in the sense of adding amplitudes: the photon has an amplitude to go one way, and an amplitude to go the other way. If the amplitudes oppose each other, the light won’t get there—even though, in this case, both holes are open.

现在，我想再补充一点关于自然界奇特现象的见解。假设我们放置一些特殊的探测器——一个在A点，一个在B点（我们可以设计一种能够检测光子是否穿过它的探测器）——这样，当两个孔都打开时，我们就能知道光子是通过哪个（哪些）孔进入的（见图50）。由于单个光子从S点到达D点的概率仅受孔间距离的影响，那么光子必然以某种巧妙的方式分裂成两个，然后再重新结合，对吧？根据这个假设，A点和B点的探测器应该总是同时发出信号（也许强度只有一半？），而D点的探测器发出信号的概率则在0到4%之间，具体数值取决于A点和B点之间的距离。

Now, here’s an extra twist to the strangeness of Nature that I’d like to tell you about. Suppose we put in some special detectors—one at A and one at B (it is possible to design a detector that can tell whether a photon went through it)—so we can tell through which hole(s) the photon goes when both holes are open (see Fig. 50). Since the probability that a single photon will get from S to D is affected only by the distance between the holes, there must be some sneaky way that the photon divides in two and then comes back together again, right? According to this hypothesis, the detectors at A and B should always go off together (at half strength, perhaps?), while the detector at D should go off with a probability of from zero to 4%, depending on the distance between A and B.

图50。当在 A 和 B 处放置特殊探测器，用于检测两个孔都打开时光线的传播方向时，实验就发生了改变。因为光子总是会穿过其中一个孔（在检查孔的位置时），所以会出现两种不同的最终结果：1）A 和 D 处的探测器都失效；2）B 和 D 处的探测器都失效。这两种情况发生的概率都约为 1%。将这两种情况的概率按常规方式相加，就得到了 D 处探测器失效的 2% 概率——如图 51(b)所示。

FIGURE 50. When special detectors are put in at A and B to tell which way the light went when both holes are open, the experiment has been changed. Because a photon always goes through one hole or the other (when you are checking the holes), there are two distinguishable final conditions: 1) the detectors at A and D go off, and 2) the detectors at B and D go off. The probability of either event happening is about 1 %. The probabilities of the two events are added in the normal way, which accounts for a 2% probability that the detector at D goes off—shown in Figure 51(b).

实际情况是这样的：A 和 B 处的探测器永远不会同时发出信号——A或B 中总会有一个发出信号。光子不会分裂成两个；它只会朝其中一个方向传播。

Here’s what actually happens: the detectors at A and B never go off together—either A or B goes off. The photon does not divide in two; it goes one way or the other.

此外，在这种情况下，D 处的探测器有 2% 的概率会失效——这恰好是 A 和 B 处概率之和 (1% + 1%)。这 2% 的失效概率不受 A 和 B 之间间距的影响；当在 A 和 B 处都放置探测器时，干扰就消失了！

Furthermore, under such conditions the detector at D goes off 2% of the time—the simple sum of the probabilities for A and B (1% + 1%). The 2% is not affected by the spacing between A and B; the interference disappears when detectors are put in at A and B!

大自然的奥秘让我们永远无法参透：如果我们用仪器测量光线的传播方向，确实可以测量出来，但奇妙的干涉效应却消失了。然而，如果我们没有仪器来测量光线的传播方向，干涉效应又会重新出现！真是太奇怪了！

Nature has got it cooked up so we’ll never be able to figure out how She does it: if we put instruments in to find out which way the light goes, we can find out, all right, but the wonderful interference effects disappear. But if we don’t have instruments that can tell which way the light goes, the interference effects come back! Very strange, indeed!

为了理解这个悖论，我想提醒大家一个最重要的原则：为了正确计算事件的概率，必须非常仔细地清晰定义整个事件——特别是实验的初始条件和最终条件。你需要在实验前后检查设备，寻找变化。当我们计算光子在A点和B点都没有探测器的情况下从S点到达D点的概率时，事件本身就是D点的探测器发出了一声咔嗒声。当D点的咔嗒声是唯一的条件变化时，我们无法判断光子的运动方向，因此存在干涉。

To understand this paradox, let me remind you of a most important principle: in order to correctly calculate the probability of an event, one must be very careful to define the complete event clearly—in particular, what the initial conditions and the final conditions of the experiment are. You look at the equipment before and after the experiment, and look for changes. When we were calculating the probability that a photon gets from S to D with no detectors at A or B, the event was, simply, the detector at D makes a click. When a click at D was the only change in conditions, there was no way to tell which way the photon went, so there was interference.

当我们在 A 和 B 点放置探测器后，问题就发生了变化。现在，我们发现存在两个完整的事件——两组最终条件——它们是可以区分的：1）A 点和 D 点的探测器都发出警报，或者 2）B 点和 D 点的探测器都发出警报。当实验中存在多种可能的最终条件时，我们必须将每种条件作为一个独立的完整事件来计算概率。

When we put in detectors at A and B, we changed the problem. Now, it turns out, there are two complete events—two sets of final conditions—that are distinguishable: 1) the detectors at A and D go off, or 2) the detectors at B and D go off. When there are a number of possible final conditions in an experiment, we must calculate the probability of each as a separate, complete event.

为了计算探测器 A 和 D 触发时的振幅，我们将代表以下各项的箭头相乘：步骤如下：一个光子从 S 点到达 A 点，另一个光子从 A 点到达 D 点，D 点的探测器发出警报。最后一个箭头的平方代表该事件发生的概率——1%——与 B 点的孔被闭合时的概率相同，因为两种情况的步骤完全相同。另一个完整的事件是 B 点和 D 点的探测器同时发出警报。该事件的概率计算方法类似，也与之前相同——约为 1%。

To calculate the amplitude that the detectors at A and D go off, we multiply the arrows that represent the following steps: a photon goes from S to A, the photon goes from A to D, and the detector at D goes off. The square of the final arrow is the probability of this event—1%—the same as when the hole at B was closed, because both cases have exactly the same steps. The other complete event is the detectors at B and D go off. The probability of this event is calculated in a similar way, and is also the same as before—about 1%.

如果我们想知道D处的探测器发出警报的频率，而不在乎是A还是B发出警报，那么概率就是这两个事件概率的简单和——2%。原则上，如果系统中还存在一些我们可以观测到的东西，从而判断光子的运动方向，那么我们就有了不同的“末态”（可区分的末态条件），我们将每个末态的概率（而不是振幅）相加¹。

If we want to know how often the detector at D goes off and we don’t care whether it was A or B that went off in the process, the probability is the simple sum of the two events—2%. In principle, if there is something left in the system that we could have observed to tell which way the photon went, we have different “final states” (distinguishable final conditions), and we add the probabilities—not the amplitudes—for each final state1.

我之所以指出这些，是因为你越了解自然界的奇异之处，就越难建立一个模型来解释哪怕是最简单的现象是如何运作的。因此，理论物理学已经放弃了这一点。

I have pointed out these things because the more you see how strangely Nature behaves, the harder it is to make a model that explains how even the simplest phenomena actually work. So theoretical physics has given up on that.

我们在第一讲中看到了如何将一个事件分解成多种不同的方式，以及如何将每种方式的箭头“相加”。在第二讲中，我们看到了如何将每种方式分解成连续的步骤，以及如何将每个步骤的箭头视为一个单位箭头的变换。以及每一步的箭头如何通过连续的收缩和转弯“倍增”。因此，我们熟悉了绘制和组合箭头（代表事件的各个部分）以获得最终箭头的所有必要规则，该最终箭头的平方就是观察到的物理事件的概率。

We saw in the first lecture how an event can be divided into alternative ways and how the arrow for each way can be “added.” In the second lecture, we saw how each way can be divided into successive steps, how the arrow for each step can be regarded as the transformation of a unit arrow, and how the arrows for each step can be “multiplied” by successive shrinks and turns. We are thus familiar with all the necessary rules for drawing and combining arrows (that represent bits and pieces of events) to obtain a final arrow, whose square is the probability of an observed physical event.

我们自然会想知道，将事件分解成越来越简单的子事件的过程究竟能推进到什么程度。事件的最小组成部分是什么？是否存在数量有限的组成部分，可以组合成所有涉及光和电子的现象？是否存在数量有限的“字母”，它们可以在量子电动力学的语言中组合成“单词”和“短语”，来描述几乎所有自然现象？

It is natural to wonder how far we can push this process of splitting events into simpler and simpler subevents. What are the smallest possible bits and pieces of events? Is there a limited number of bits and pieces that can be compounded to form all the phenomena that involve light and electrons? Is there a limited number of “letters” in this language of quantum electrodynamics that can be combined to form “words” and “phrases” that describe nearly every phenomenon of Nature?

图51.当 A 或 B 处没有探测器时，会产生干扰——光量在 0% 到 4% 之间变化 (a)。当 A 和 B 处的探测器 100% 可靠时，不会产生干扰——到达 D 的光量恒定为 2% (b)。当 A 和 B 处的探测器并非 100% 可靠时（即，有时A或 B 处没有任何可探测的信号），则会出现三种可能的最终情况——A和 D 都熄灭，B 和 D 都熄灭，以及只有 D 熄灭。因此，最终曲线是混合曲线，由每种可能的最终情况的贡献构成。当 A 和 B 处的探测器可靠性较低时，干扰会更大。因此，情况 (c) 中的探测器可靠性低于情况 (d)。关于干扰的原理是：必须通过累加箭头并对最后一个箭头的长度进行平方来独立计算每种不同最终情况的概率；之后，将各个概率按常规方式相加。

FIGURE 51. When there are no detectors at A or B, there is interference—the amount of light varies from zero to 4% (a). When there are detectors at A and B that are 100% reliable, there is no interference—the amount of light reaching D is a constant 2% (b). When the detectors at A and B are not 100% reliable (i.e., when sometimes there is nothing left in A or in B that can be detected), there are now three possible final conditions—A and D go off, B and D go off, and D goes off alone. The final curve is thus a mixture, made up of contributions from each possible final condition. When the detectors at A and B are less reliable, there is more interference present. Thus the detectors in case (c) are less reliable than in case (d). The principle regarding interference is: The probability of each of the different possible final conditions must be independently calculated by adding arrows and squaring the length of the final arrow; after that, the several probabilities are added together in the normal fashion.

答案是肯定的；数字是三。产生所有与光和电子相关的现象只需要三个基本动作。

The answer is yes; the number is three. There are only three basic actions needed to produce all of the phenomena associated with light and electrons.

在讲解这三种基本作用之前，我应该先正式介绍一下构成物理过程的粒子。这些粒子是光子和电子。光子，也就是光的粒子，在前两讲中已经详细讨论过。电子是在1895年被发现的：人们可以数出它们的数量；也可以把一个电子放在油滴上，测量它的电荷。人们逐渐认识到，这些粒子的运动就是导线中电流的来源。

Before I tell you what these three basic actions are, I should properly introduce you to the actors. The actors are photons and electrons. The photons, particles of light, have been discussed at length in the first two lectures. Electrons were discovered in 1895 as particles: you could count them; you could put one of them on an oil drop and measure its electric charge. It gradually became apparent that the motion of these particles accounted for electricity in wires.

电子被发现后不久，人们认为原子就像一个个小型太阳系，由一个中心较重的原子核和围绕原子核运行的电子组成，就像行星绕太阳运行一样。如果你还认为原子就是这样，那你还停留在1910年。1924年，路易·德布罗意发现电子具有波动性。不久之后，贝尔实验室的C·J·戴维森和L·H·革末用电子轰击镍晶体，发现电子也会以极快的角度反弹（就像X射线一样），而且这些角度可以根据德布罗意提出的电子波长公式计算出来。

Shortly after electrons were discovered it was thought that atoms were like little solar systems, made up of a central, heavy part (called the nucleus) and electrons, which went around in “orbits,” much like the planets do when they go around the sun. If you think that’s the way atoms are, then you’re back in 1910. In 1924 Louis De Broglie found that there was a wavelike character associated with electrons, and soon afterwards, C. J. Davisson and L. H. Germer of the Bell Laboratories bombarded a nickel crystal with electrons and showed that they, too, bounced off at crazy angles (just like X-rays do), and that these angles could be calculated from De Broglie’s formula for the wavelength of an electron.

当我们从大尺度观察光子时——远大于秒表转动一圈所需的距离——我们所观察到的现象可以用诸如“光沿直线传播”之类的规则很好地近似描述，因为在最短时间路径周围存在足够多的路径来强化这一规律。彼此之间，以及其他足够多的路径，足以相互抵消。但是，当光子运动的空间变得太小时（例如屏幕上的微小孔洞），这些规则就失效了——我们发现光不一定沿直线传播，两个孔洞之间会产生干涉，等等。电子的情况也是如此：在大尺度上，它们像粒子一样沿着确定的路径运动。但在小尺度上，例如原子内部，空间非常小，以至于没有主路径，也没有“轨道”；电子可以沿着各种各样的路径运动，每种路径都有其振幅。干涉现象变得非常重要，我们必须将所有路径的振幅相加才能预测电子可能出现的位置。

When we look at photons on a large scale—much larger than the distance required for one stopwatch turn—the phenomena that we see are very well approximated by rules such as “light travels in straight lines,” because there are enough paths around the path of minimum time to reinforce each other, and enough other paths to cancel each other out. But when the space through which a photon moves becomes too small (such as the tiny holes in the screen), these rules fail—we discover that light doesn’t have to go in straight lines, there are interferences created by two holes, and so on. The same situation exists with electrons: when seen on a large scale, they travel like particles, on definite paths. But on a small scale, such as inside an atom, the space is so small that there is no main path, no “orbit”; there are all sorts of ways the electron could go, each with an amplitude. The phenomenon of interference becomes very important, and we have to sum the arrows to predict where an electron is likely to be.

值得注意的是，电子最初看起来像粒子，后来才发现它们具有波动性。另一方面，除了牛顿误认为光是“粒子”之外，光最初看起来像波，后来才发现它具有粒子性。事实上，这两种物体都兼具波和粒子的特性。为了避免创造诸如“波粒”之类的新词，我们选择称它们为“粒子”，但我们都知道它们遵循我一直在解释的绘制和组合箭头的规则。自然界中所有的“粒子”——夸克、胶子、中微子等等（将在下一讲讨论）——似乎都以这种量子力学的方式运行。

It’s rather interesting to note that electrons looked like particles at first, and their wavish character was later discovered. On the other hand, apart from Newton making a mistake and thinking that light was “corpuscular,” light looked like waves at first, and its characteristics as a particle were discovered later. In fact, both objects behave somewhat like waves, and somewhat like particles. In order to save ourselves from inventing new words such as “wavicles,” we have chosen to call these objects “particles,” but we all know that they obey these rules for drawing and combining arrows that I have been explaining. It appears that all the “particles” in Nature—quarks, gluons, neutrinos, and so forth (which will be discussed in the next lecture)—behave in this quantum mechanical way.

现在，我向你们介绍三种基本作用，光和电子的所有现象都由此产生。

So now, I present to you the three basic actions, from which all the phenomena of light and electrons arise.

—A动作#1：光子从一个地方运动到另一个地方。

—ACTION #1: A photon goes from place to place.

—动作#2：电子从一个地方移动到另一个地方。

—ACTION #2: An electron goes from place to place.

—动作#3：电子发射或吸收光子。

—ACTION #3: An electron emits or absorbs a photon.

这些动作中的每一个都有一个幅度——一个箭头——可以根据某些规则计算出来。稍后我会告诉你们这些规则或定律，我们可以用它们来……整个世界（除了原子核和引力，一如既往！）。

Each of these actions has an amplitude—an arrow—that can be calculated according to certain rules. In a moment, I’ll tell you those rules, or laws, out of which we can make the whole world (aside from the nuclei, and gravitation, as always!).

现在，这些事件发生的舞台不仅仅是空间，而是空间和时间。到目前为止，我一直忽略了与时间相关的问题，例如光子究竟何时离开光源，又何时到达探测器。尽管空间实际上是三维的，但在我即将绘制的图表中，我将把它简化为一维：横轴表示特定物体在空间中的位置，纵轴表示时间。

Now, the stage on which these actions take place is not just space, it is space and time. Until now, I have disregarded problems concerning time, such as exactly when a photon leaves the source and exactly when it arrives at the detector. Although space is really three-dimensional, I’m going to reduce it to one dimension on the graphs that I’m going to draw: I will show a particular object’s location in space on the horizontal axis, and the time on the vertical axis.

我要在时空（或者我可能无意中称之为时空）中绘制的第一个事件是一个静止的棒球（见图52）。在星期四早上，我将其标记为 T₀ ，棒球占据了某个空间，我将其标记为 X₀ 。片刻之后，在 T₁ ，它仍然占据着相同的空间，因为它静止不动。再过片刻，在 T₂ ，棒球仍然位于 X₀ 。因此，静止的棒球的示意图是一条垂直向上延伸的带状区域，其内部布满了棒球。

The first event I am going to draw in space and time—or space-time, as I might inadvertently call it—is a baseball standing still (See Fig. 52). On Thursday morning, which I will label as T0, the baseball occupies a certain space, which I will label as X0. A few moments later, at T1, it occupies the same space, because it’s standing still. A few moments later, at T2, the baseball is still at X0. So the diagram of a baseball standing still is a vertical band, going straight up, with baseball all over it inside.

图52.宇宙中所有活动发生的舞台是时空。时空通常由四个维度构成（三个空间维度和一个时间维度），但在这里我们将用二维来表示——一个代表空间，位于水平方向；一个代表时间，位于垂直方向。每次我们观察棒球时（例如在 T3 时刻），它都位于同一个位置。随着时间的推移，这便形成了一个向上延伸的“棒球带”。

FIGURE 52. The stage on which all actions in the universe take place is space-time. Usually consisting of four dimensions (three for space and one for time), space-time will be represented here in two dimensions—one for space, in the horizontal dimension, and one for time, in the vertical. Each time we look at the baseball (such as at time T3), it is in the same place. This produces a “band of baseball” going straight up, as time goes on.

如果一个棒球在失重的外太空中漂浮，径直朝墙壁飞去，会发生什么？嗯……周四早上（T0 ） ，棒球从X0点开始运动（见图53），但过了一会儿，它的位置就变了——它漂移了一点，到了X1点。随着棒球继续漂移，它在时空图上形成了一个倾斜的“棒球带”。当棒球击中墙壁（墙壁静止不动，因此是一条垂直的带状物）时，它会原路返回，回到它在空间中的起始点（X0 ），但时间点已经不同（T6 ）。

What happens if we have a baseball drifting in the weightlessness of outer space, going straight toward a wall? Well, on Thursday morning (T0) it starts at X0 (see Fig. 53), but a little bit later, it’s not in the same place—it has drifted over a little bit, to X1 As the baseball continues to drift, it creates a slanted “band of baseball” on the diagram of space-time. When the baseball hits the wall (which is standing still and is therefore a vertical band), it goes back the other way, exactly where it came from in space (X0), but to a different point in time (T6).

图53.一个棒球垂直向墙壁飘去，然后反弹回其原始位置（如图下方所示），它在一维空间中运动，看起来像一条倾斜的“棒球带”。在 T1 和 T2 时刻，棒球越来越靠近墙壁；在 T3 时刻，它撞到墙壁，然后开始反弹。

FIGURE 53. A baseball drifting directly toward a wall at right angles and then bouncing back to its original location (shown below the graph) is moving in one dimension and appears as a slanted “band of baseball.” At times T1 and T2, the baseball is getting closer to the wall; at T3 it hits the wall, and begins to go back.

至于时间尺度，最方便的做法不是用秒，而是用更小的单位来表示时间。由于我们要处理的是运动速度极快的光子和电子，我将用45°角来表示以光速运动的物体。例如，对于一个以光速从X₁T₁运动到X₂T₂的粒子， X₁和X₂之间的水平距离等于T₁和T₂之间的垂直距离（见图54）。时间被拉伸的倍数（即用45°角来表示以光速运动的粒子）称为c，你会发现c在爱因斯坦的公式中随处可见——这是因为人们不幸地选择了秒作为时间单位，而不是光行进一米所需的时间。

As for the time scale, it is most convenient to represent the time not in seconds, but in much smaller units. Since we will be dealing with photons and electrons, which move very rapidly, I am going to have a 45° angle represent something going the speed of light. For example, for a particle moving at the speed of light from X1T1 to X2T2,the horizontal distance between X1 and X2 is the same as the vertical distance between T1 and T2 (see Fig. 54). The factor by which time is stretched out (to make a 45° angle represent a particle going the speed of light) is called c, and you’ll find c’s flying around everywhere in Einstein’s formulas—they are the result of the unfortunate choice of the second as the unit of time, rather than the time it takes light to go one meter.

现在，让我们详细看一下第一个基本作用——光子。光子从一个地方运动到另一个地方。我姑且用一条从A到B的曲线来表示这个运动，其实并没有什么特别的原因。我应该更严谨一些：应该说，一个已知在某一时刻位于某一位置的光子，具有一定的振幅，可以在另一个时刻到达另一位置。在我的时空图（见图55）上，位于A点（X <sub>1 </sub>，T<sub> 1</sub> ）的光子具有一定的振幅，可以出现在B点（X <sub>2 </sub>，T<sub> 2</sub>）。我将这个振幅的大小记为P(A到B)。

Now, let’s look at the first basic action in detail—a photon goes from place to place. I will draw this action as a wiggly line from A to B for no good reason. I should be more careful: I should say, a photon that is known to be at a given place at a given time has a certain amplitude to get to another place at another time. On my space-time graph (see Fig. 55), the photon at point A—at X1 and T1—has an amplitude to appear at point B—X2 and T2. The size of this amplitude I will call P(A to B).

图54。我将在这些图表中使用的时间尺度将显示以光速运动的粒子在时空中以 45 度角运动。光传播 30 厘米（从 X1到X2或从X2到X1 ）所需的时间约为十亿分之一秒。

FIGURE 54. The time scale I will use in these graphs will show particles going at the speed of light to be travelling at a 45-degree angle through space-time. The amount of time it takes light to go 30 centimeters—from X1 to X2 or from X2 to X1—is about one-billionth of a second.

图55.一个光子（用波浪线表示）具有从时空中的点 A 传播到另一点 B 的振幅。这个振幅，我称之为 P(A 到 B)，可以通过一个仅取决于位置差 (X₂ – X₁) 和时间差 (T₂ – T₁) 的公式计算得出。实际上，它是一个简单的函数，是它们平方差的倒数——一个“区间”I，可以写成 (X₂ – X₁ ) ² – ( T₂ – T₁ ) ²。

FIGURE 55. A photon (represented by a wavy line) has an amplitude to go from a point A in space-time to another point, B. This amplitude, which I will call P(A to B), is calculated from a formula that depends only on the difference in location—(X2 – X1)—and the difference of the time—(T2 – T1). In fact, it’s a simple function that is the inverse of the difference of their squares—an “interval,” I, that can be written as (X2 – X1)2 – (T2 – T1)2.

箭头 P(A 到 B) 的长度有一个公式。这是自然界的一条伟大法则，而且非常简单。它取决于两点之间的距离差和时间差。这些差值可以用数学公式2表示为 (X 2 – X 1 ) 和 (T 2 – T 1 )。

There is a formula for the size of this arrow, P(A to B). It is one of the great laws of Nature, and it’s very simple. It depends on the difference in distance and the difference in time between the two points. These differences can be expressed mathematically2 as (X2 – X1) and (T2 – T1).

P(A到B)的主要贡献发生在光速为常规速度时——即(X₂ – X₁)等于(T₂ – T₁)时——人们通常会认为所有事件都会发生在这里，但光速也可能超过（或低于）常规光速。你在上节课中了解到光并非只沿直线传播；现在，你又了解到光速并非只以光速传播！

The major contribution to P(A to B) occurs at the conventional speed of light—when (X2 – X1 is equal to (T2 – T1)—where one would expect it all to occur, but there is also an amplitude for light to go faster (or slower) than the conventional speed of light. You found out that in the last lecture that light doesn’t go only in straight lines; now, you find out that it doesn’t go only at the speed of light!

你可能会惊讶地发现，光子的速度存在快于或慢于常规速度 c 的可能性。这些可能性的幅度与速度 c 的贡献相比非常小；事实上，当光传播很远的距离时，它们会相互抵消。然而，当距离很短时——例如在许多情况下——我将绘制的图表——这些其他可能性变得至关重要，必须加以考虑。

It may surprise you that there is an amplitude for a photon to go at speeds faster or slower than the conventional speed, c. The amplitudes for these possibilities are very small compared to the contribution from speed c; in fact, they cancel out when light travels over long distances. However, when the distances are short—as in many of the diagrams I will be drawing—these other possibilities become vitally important and must be considered.

所以，这就是第一个基本作用，物理学的第一条基本定律——光子从一点传播到另一点。这解释了光学的一切；这就是光理论的全部！嗯，也不完全是：我（一如既往地）省略了偏振，以及光与物质的相互作用，这就引出了第二定律。

So that’s the first basic action, the first basic law of physics—a photon goes from point to point. That explains all about optics; that’s the entire theory of light! Well, not quite: I left out polarization (as always), and the interaction of light with matter, which brings me to the second law.

图56。当光速为 C 时，“间隔”I 等于零，此时光在 12 点钟方向的贡献最大。当 I 大于零时，光在 3 点钟方向的贡献较小，且与 I 成反比；当 I 小于零时，光在 9 点钟方向的贡献也较小。因此，光速可以超过或低于 C，但这些振幅在长距离传播过程中会相互抵消。

FIGURE 56. When light goes at the speed C, the “interval,” I, equals zero, and there is a large contribution in the 12 o’clock direction. When I is greater than zero, there is a small contribution in the three o’clock direction inversely proportional to I; when I is less than zero, there is a similar contribution in the nine o’clock direction. Thus light has an amplitude to go faster or slower than speed C, but these amplitudes cancel out over long distances.

量子电动力学的第二个基本作用是：一个电子在时空中从A点运动到B点。（目前，我们将这个电子想象成一个简化的、虚拟的电子，没有极化——物理学家称之为“自旋零”电子。实际上，电子具有某种极化，但这并不会给主要概念增添任何内容；它只是让公式稍微复杂一些。）这个作用的振幅公式，我称之为E(A到B)，也取决于(X₂ – X₁ )和(T₂ – T₁ )（与注释2中描述的组合相同），以及一个我称之为“ n ”的数。一旦确定了n，我们所有的计算结果就能与实验结果相符。（我们稍后会看到如何确定n的值。）这是一个相当复杂的公式，很抱歉，我不知道如何推导。简单来说，你或许会感兴趣：当 n 设为零时，光子在时空中从一点运动到另一点的 P(A 到 B) 公式与电子在时空中从一点运动到另一点的 E(A 到 B)公式相同。3

The second action fundamental to quantum electrodynamics is: An electron goes from point A to point B in space-time. (For the moment we will imagine this electron as a simplified, fake electron, with no polarization—what the physicists call a “spin-zero” electron. In reality, electrons have a type of polarization, which doesn’t add anything to the main ideas; it only complicates the formulas a little bit.) The formula for the amplitude for this action, which I will call E(A to B) also depends on (X2 – X1 and (T2 – T1 (in the same combination as described in note 2) as well as on a number I will call “n,” a number that, once determined, enables all our calculations to agree with experiment. (We will see later how we determine n’s value.) It is a rather complicated formula, and I’m sorry that I don’t know how to explain it in simple terms. However, you might be interested to know that the formula for P(A to B)—a photon going from place to place in space-time—is the same as that for E(A to B)—an electron going from place to place—if n is set to zero.3

第三个基本动作是：电子发射或吸收光子——发射或吸收哪个光子都无所谓。我将这个动作称为“连接”或“耦合”。为了在我的图中区分电子和光子，我将每个穿过时空的电子都画成一条直线。因此，每一次耦合都是两条直线和一条波浪线之间的连接（见图58）。电子发射或吸收光子的振幅没有复杂的公式；它不依赖于任何因素——它只是一个数值！我将这个连接数称为j——它的值约为-0.1：收缩到大约十分之一，半圈。4

The third basic action is: an electron emits or absorbs a photon—it doesn’t make any difference which. I will call this action a “junction,” or “coupling.” To distinguish electrons from photons in my diagrams, I will draw each electron going through space-time as a straight line. Every coupling, therefore, is a junction between two straight lines and a wavy line (see Fig. 58). There is no complicated formula for the amplitude of an electron to emit or absorb a photon; it doesn’t depend on anything—it’s just a number! This junction number I will call j—its value is about –0.1: a shrink to about one-tenth, and half a turn.4

好了，这些基本操作就这些——除了由于这种两极分化而产生的一些细微复杂情况，而我们总是忽略这些情况。接下来，我们要把这三个操作结合起来，来描述一些更为复杂的情况。

Well, that’s all there is to these basic actions—except for some slight complications due to this polarization that we’re always leaving out. Our next job is to put these three actions together to represent circumstances that are somewhat more complicated.

图57.电子在时空中从一点到另一点具有一定的振幅，我将其称为“E(A 到 B)”。虽然我将E (A 到 B) 表示为两点之间的直线 (a)，但我们可以将其视为多个振幅 (b) 的总和——其中包括电子在“两跳”路径上的 C 点或 C' 点改变方向的振幅，以及在“三跳”路径上的 D 点和 E 点改变方向的振幅——此外还包括从 A 到 B 的直接路径。电子改变方向的次数可以从零到无穷大，电子在时空中从 A 到 B 的路径上可以改变方向的点也是无穷多个。所有这些点都包含在 E(A 到 B) 中。

FIGURE 57. An electron has an amplitude to go from point to point in space-time, which I will call “E(A to B).” Although I will represent E(A to B) as a straight line between two points (a), we can think of it as the sum of many amplitudes (b)—among them, the amplitude for the electron to change direction at points C or C on a “two-hop” path, and the amplitude to change direction at D and E on a “three-hop” path—in addition to the direct path from A to B. The number of times an electron can change direction is anywhere from zero to infinity, and the points at which the electron can change direction on its way from A to B in space-time are infinite. All are included in E (A to B).

图58.图中用直线表示的电子具有发射或吸收光子的特定振幅（用波浪线表示）。由于发射和吸收的振幅相同，我将这两种情况都称为“耦合”。耦合的振幅是一个数值，我称之为j；对于电子而言，j 值约为 -0.1（这个数值有时被称为“电荷”）。

FIGURE 58. An electron, depicted by a straight line, has a certain amplitude to emit or absorb a photon, shown by a wavy line. Since the amplitude to emit or absorb is the same, I will call either case a “coupling.” The amplitude for a coupling is a number that I will call j; it is about –0.1 for the electron (this number is sometimes called the “charge”).

第一个例子，我们来计算时空中的点 1 和点 2 处的两个电子最终到达同一点的概率。点 3 和点 4（见图59）。此事件可通过多种方式发生。第一种方式是位置 1 的电子跃迁到位置 3——通过将 1 和 3 代入公式 E(A 到 B) 计算得出，我将其记为 E(1 到 3)——同时位置 2 的电子跃迁到位置 4——通过 E(2 到 4) 计算得出。这是两个同时发生的“子事件”，因此将这两个箭头相乘即可得到表示该事件第一种方式的箭头。因此，我们将“第一种方式箭头”的公式写为 E(1 到 3) * E(2 到 4)。

For our first example, let’s calculate the probability that two electrons, at points 1 and 2 in space-time, end up at points 3 and 4 (see Fig. 59). This event can happen in several ways. The first way is that the electron at 1 goes to 3—computed by putting 1 and 3 into the formula E(A to B), which I will write as E(1 to 3)—and the electron at 2 goes to 4—computed by E(2 to 4). These are two “subevents” happening concomitantly, so the two arrows are multiplied to produce an arrow for this first way the event could happen. Therefore we write the formula for the “first-way arrow” as E(1 to 3) * E(2 to 4).

图59。为了计算时空中点 1 和点 2 的电子最终到达点 3 和点 4 的概率，我们首先利用 E(A 到 B) 的公式计算电子从点 1 到点 3 以及从点 2 到点 4 的“第一路径”箭头；然后计算电子从点 1 到点 4 以及从点 2 到点 3 的“第二路径”箭头（即“交叉”路径）。最后，我们将“第一路径”和“第二路径”箭头相加，得到最终箭头的近似值。（这适用于假设的、简化的“自旋为零”的电子。如果我们考虑电子的极化，则需要将这两个箭头相减，而不是相加。）

FIGURE 59. To calculate the probability that electrons at points 1 and 2 in space-time end up at points 3 and 4, we calculate the “first way” arrow for 1 going to 3 and 2 going to 4 with the formula for E(A to B); then we calculate the “second way” arrow for 1 going to 4 and 2 going to 3 (a “crossover”). Finally, we add the “first way” and “second way” arrows to arrive at a good approximation of the final arrow. (This is true for the fake, simplified “spin zero” electron. Had we included the polarization of the electron, we would have subtracted—rather than added—the two arrows.)

该事件发生的另一种方式是，位置 1 的电子跃迁到位置 4，位置 2 的电子跃迁到位置 3——同样，这是两个同时发生的子事件。“第二种路径箭头”是 E(1 到 4) * E(2 到 3)，我们将其添加到“第一种路径”箭头中。5

Another way this event could happen is that the electron at 1 goes to 4 and the electron at 2 goes to 3—again, two concomitant subevents. The “second-way arrow” is E(1 to 4) * E(2 to 3), and we add it to the “first-way” arrow.5

这是对该事件振幅的一个很好的近似值。为了进行更精确的计算，使其与实验结果更吻合，我们必须考虑……该事件发生的其他方式。例如，对于该事件发生的两种主要方式，一个电子可以带电移动到某个新的奇妙位置并发射一个光子（见图60）。同时，另一个电子可以移动到其他位置并吸收该光子。计算第一种新方式的振幅需要将以下振幅相乘：电子从 1 移动到新的奇妙位置 5（在那里它发射一个光子），然后再从 5 移动到 3；另一个电子从位置 2 移动到位置 6（在那里它吸收了光子），然后从位置 6 移动到位置 4。我们必须记住要包含光子从位置 5 移动到位置 6 的振幅。我将用一种高深的数学方式来表示这种事件发生的振幅，你可以跟着做：E(1 到 5)* j *E(5 到 3) * E(2 到 6)* j *E(6 到 4) * P(5 到 6)——大量的收缩和旋转。（至于另一种情况的符号，即位置 1 的电子最终到达位置 4，位置 2 的电子最终到达位置 3，就留给你自己去理解了。）6

This is a good approximation for the amplitude of this event. To make a more exact calculation that will agree more closely with the results of experiment, we must consider other ways this event could happen. For instance, for each of the two main ways the event can happen, one electron could go charging off to some new and wonderful place and emit a photon (see Fig. 60). Meanwhile, the other electron could go to some other place and absorb the photon. Calculating the amplitude for the first of these new ways involves multiplying the amplitudes for: an electron goes from 1 to the new and wonderful place, 5 (where it emits a photon), and then goes from 5 to 3; the other electron goes from 2 to the other place, 6 (where it absorbs the photon), and then goes from 6 to 4. We must remember to include the amplitude that the photon goes from 5 to 6. I’m going to write the amplitude for this way the event could happen in a high-class mathematical fashion, and you can follow along: E(1 to 5)*j*E(5 to 3) * E(2 to 6)*j*E(6 to 4) * P(5 to 6)—a lot of shrinking and turning. (I’ll let you figure out the notation for the other case, where the electron at 1 ends up at 4, and the electron at 2 ends up at 3.)6

图60展示了图 59中事件发生的两种“其他方式”：在每种情况下，光子均在 5 处发射，并在 6 处被吸收。这些替代方案的最终条件与其他情况相同——两个电子进入，两个电子离开——并且这些结果与其他替代方案无法区分。因此，必须将这些“其他方式”的箭头添加到图 59中的箭头上，才能更好地近似表示该事件的最终箭头。

FIGURE 60. Two “other ways” the event in Fig. 59 could happen are: a photon is emitted at 5 and absorbed at 6 for each case. The final conditions of these alternatives are the same as for the other cases—two electrons went in, and two electrons came out—and these results are indistinguishable from the other alternatives. Therefore the arrows for these “other ways” must be added to the arrows in Fig. 59 to arrive at a better approximation of the final arrow for the event.

等等：位置 5 和位置 6 可能出现在空间和时间的任何位置——没错，任何位置——所有这些位置的箭头都必须计算出来并加在一起。你看，这工作量可不小。倒不是说规则有多难——就像下跳棋一样：规则很简单，但要反复使用。所以，我们计算的难点在于要把这么多箭头加在一起。这就是为什么研究生要花四年时间才能学会如何高效地完成这项工作——而我们现在讨论的还只是个简单的问题！（如果问题太难，我们就直接交给电脑！）

But wait: positions 5 and 6 could be anywhere in space and time—yes, anywhere—and the arrows for all of those positions have to be calculated and added together. You see it’s getting to be a lot of work. Not that the rules are so difficult—it’s like playing checkers: the rules are simple, but you use them over and over. So our difficulty in calculating comes from having to pile so many arrows together. That’s why it takes four years of graduate work for the students to learn how to do this efficiently—and we’re looking at an easy problem! (When the problems get too difficult, we just put them on the computer!)

我想指出一些关于光子发射和吸收的问题：如果第 6 点晚于第 5 点，我们可以说光子在第 5 点发射，在第 6 点被吸收（见图61）。如果第 6 点早于第 5 点，我们可能更倾向于说光子在第 6 点发射，在第 5 点被吸收，但我们也可以说光子在时间上逆向运动！然而，我们不必担心光子在时空中的运动方向；这一切都包含在 P(5 到 6) 的公式中，我们称之为光子“交换”。大自然的简洁之美，岂不令人惊叹！7

I would like to point out something about photons being emitted and absorbed: if point 6 is later than point 5, we might say that the photon was emitted at 5 and absorbed at 6 (see Fig. 61). If 6 is earlier than 5, we might prefer to say the photon was emitted at 6 and absorbed at 5, but we could just as well say that the photon is going backwards in time! However, we don’t have to worry about which way in space-time the photon went; it’s all included in the formula for P(5 to 6), and we say a photon was “exchanged.” Isn’t it beautiful how simple Nature is!7

现在，除了在 5 和 6 之间交换的光子之外，还可以在 7 和 8 这两个点之间交换另一个光子（见图62）。我太累了，没法把所有需要相乘的基本作用都写下来，但是——你可能已经注意到了——每条直线都有一个 E(A 到 B)，每条波浪线都有一个 P(A 到 B)，每个耦合都有一个j 。因此，对于所有可能的 5、6、7 和 8，都有六个 E(A 到 B)，两个 P(A 到 B) 和四个j ！这就产生了数十亿个需要相乘然后相加的小箭头！

Now, in addition to the photon that is exchanged between 5 and 6, another photon could be exchanged—between two points, 7 and 8 (see Fig. 62). I’m too tired to write down all the basic actions whose arrows have to be multiplied, but—as you may have noticed—every straight line gets an E(A to B), every wavy line gets a P(A to B), and every coupling gets a j. Thus, there are six E(A to B)’s, two P(A to B)’s, and four j’s—for every possible 5, 6, 7, and 8! That makes billions of tiny arrows that have to be multiplied and then added together!

图61.由于光的振幅可以超过或低于常规光速，因此上述三个例子中的光子都可以被认为是从点 5 发射并在点 6 被吸收，即使例子 (b) 中的光子是同时发射和被吸收的，而例子 (c) 中的光子是晚于被吸收的——在这种情况下，你可能更愿意说它是从点 6 发射并被点 5 吸收的；否则，光子就必须逆向运动！就计算（以及自然）而言，这都是一样的（而且都是可能的），所以我们只需说一个光子发生了“交换”，并将时空中的位置代入 P(A 到 B) 的公式即可。

FIGURE 61. Since light has an amplitude to go faster or slower than the conventional speed of light, the photons in all three examples above can be thought of as being emitted from point 5 and absorbed at point 6, even though the photon in example (b) is emitted at the same time that it is absorbed, and the photon in (c) is emitted later than it is absorbed—a situation in which you might have preferred to say that it was emitted by 6 and absorbed by 5; otherwise, the photon would have to go backwards in time! As far as calculating (and Nature) is concerned, it’s all the same (and it’s all possible), so we simply say a photon is “exchanged” and plug the locations in space-time into the formula for P(A to B).

图62.图59所示事件发生的另一种方式是两个光子交换。这种方式有很多种可能的形式（我们稍后会详细讨论）；这里展示其中一种。这种方式的箭头包含了所有可能的中间点 5、6、7 和 8，计算起来非常困难。由于j小于 0.1，与图 59中不包含 j 的“第一种方式”和“第二种方式”的箭头相比，这条箭头的长度通常小于万分之一（因为涉及四个耦合）。

FIGURE 62. Yet another way the event in Fig. 59 could happen is that two photons could be exchanged. Many diagrams of this way are possible (as we will see in more detail later); one of them is shown here. The arrow for this way involves all possible intermediate points 5, 6, 7, and 8, and is calculated with great difficulty. Because j is less than 0.1, the length of this arrow is generally less than 1 part in 10,000 (because there are four couplings involved) compared to the “first way” and “second way” arrows in Fig. 59 that contained no j’s.

计算这个简单事件的振幅似乎是一件毫无希望的事情，但作为一名研究生，你必须拿到学位，所以你只能继续努力。

It appears that calculating the amplitude for this simple event is a hopeless business, but when you’re a graduate student you’ve got to get your degree, so you keep on going.

但成功并非没有希望。希望就藏在那个神奇的数字 j 中。事件发生的最初两种方式都与太空有关。计算中没有j ；下一种方法有j * j，而我们之前讨论的最后一种方法是j * j * j * j。由于j * j小于 0.01，这意味着这种方法的箭头长度通常小于前两种方法的箭头长度的 1%；包含j * j * j * j的箭头长度小于 1% 的 1%——也就是万分之一——与不包含j的箭头相比。如果你有足够的时间在电脑上，你可以计算出所有包含j为6 的情况——也就是百万分之一——并达到与实验相同的精度。这就是简单事件计算的方法。就是这样，仅此而已！

But there is hope for success. It is found in that magic number, j. The first two ways the event could happen had Space no j’s in the calculation; the next way had j*j, and the last way we looked at had j*j*j*j. Since j*j is less than 0.01, it means the length of the arrow for this way is generally less than 1% of the arrow for the first two ways; an arrow with j*j*j*j in it is less than 1% of 1%—one part in 10,000—compared to the arrows that have no j. If you’ve got enough time on the computer, you can work out the possibilities that involve j6—one part in a million—and match the accuracy of the experiments. That’s how the calculations of simple events are made. That’s the way it works; that’s all there is to it!

现在我们来看另一个事件。事件开始时有一个光子和一个电子，结束时也仍然是一个光子和一个电子。这种事件发生的一种方式是：一个光子被电子吸收，电子继续运动一段距离，然后释放出一个新的光子。这个过程称为光的散射。在绘制散射图和进行相关计算时，我们必须考虑一些特殊情况（见图63）。例如，电子可能在吸收光子之前就发射出一个光子（b）。更奇怪的是，还有一种可能性。(c) 电子发射一个光子，然后逆时间运动吸收另一个光子，之后再次顺时间运动。这种“逆向运动”电子的路径可能非常长，以至于在实验室的实际物理实验中看起来是真实的。这种行为包含在这些图表和 E(A 到 B) 的方程中。

Let’s look at another event now. We begin with a photon and an electron, and we end with a photon and an electron. One way this event can happen is: a photon is absorbed by an electron, the electron continues on a bit, and a new photon comes out. This process is called the scattering of light. When we make the diagrams and calculations for scattering, we must include some peculiar possibilities (see Fig. 63). For example, the electron could emit a photon before absorbing one (b). Even more strange is the possibility (c) that the electron emits a photon, then travels backwards in time to absorb a photon, and then proceeds forwards in time again. The path of such a “backwards-moving” electron can be so long as to appear real in an actual physical experiment in the laboratory. Its behavior is included in these diagrams and the equation for E(A to B).

图63.光的散射涉及一个光子进入电子，然后一个光子射出电子——顺序不一定如此，如例 (b) 所示。例 (c) 展示了一种奇特但真实存在的可能性：电子发射一个光子，然后逆时间方向快速运动以吸收另一个光子，之后继续顺时间方向运动。
